entry.title: iVolve Series | Part D : Beyond Go-Live
entry.content:
Note: This blog is a continuation of The iVolve Series | Your Integration Estate Is Holding You Back. Well, It Doesn’t Have To. Read iVolve Series | Part C: Automate Everything before continuing.

Go-live is not the end of an integration modernisation programme. It is the beginning of a new operating model. For many organisations, the transition from legacy middleware to SAP Integration Suite on BTP represents the first time their integration landscape has been managed with cloud-native DevOps practices i.e. continuous deployment, automated governance, proactive monitoring, and genuine high availability.

The challenge is that SAP Integration Suite, while a powerful platform, does not come with enterprise-grade DevOps tooling out of the box. Organisations that invest in a migration without investing in the operational layer often find themselves replicating the operational debt of the legacy landscape on a newer platform.

Tarento's OpEase Suite was built to close this gap. This blog examines the four key components and how they work together to deliver a resilient, well-governed, AI-enhanced integration operations environment.

 

OneFailover : Near-Zero Downtime for Critical Landscapes

For large integration landscapes handling business-critical transactions, planned and unplanned downtime is not just an IT inconvenience; it is a business risk with measurable financial impact. SAP Integration Suite maintenance windows, regional incidents, or platform upgrades can require traffic to be rerouted to a standby environment. Without tooling, this is a manual, error-prone process that takes hours and relies on precise coordination across multiple teams.

OneFailover provides an operational layer that standardises and automates this process entirely.

How OneFailover Works

OneFailover continuously maintains a failover tenant or runtime in a 'ready state' fully synchronised with the primary environment, tested, and prepared for immediate cutover. The core capabilities span several dimensions:

  • Sync & Orchestration: Full-scope synchronisation of iFlows, Message Mappings, Value Mappings, and Scripts across tenants, with dependency-aware ordering and group/sub-group priorities
  • Failover Control: Group-based methodology with deployment prioritisation and manual listener cutover, using OneFailover Profiles to map primary-to-failover configurations
  • Backup & Restore: GitHub-based, artefact-level versioning with selective restore capability and overwrite guardrails
  • Runtime Awareness: Edge Cell and multi-runtime/tenant awareness, with a persistence layer to inventory iFlows and tenant metadata
  • Observability: Operational Status and Monitoring dashboards, Readiness scores, activity and execution logs, and throughput optimisations
  • Security: User Credentials, Global Variables, Number Ranges, and Access Policies included in sync and backup, with secret-safe metadata handling

     

OneFailover integrates with enterprise routing patterns including Azure Traffic Manager, internal DNS solutions like F5 or Citrix NetScaler, and GitHub for version control, fitting naturally into existing enterprise infrastructure.

 

When to Use OneFailover

OneFailover is relevant across four operational scenarios: planned maintenance windows where manual coordination effort needs to be eliminated; regional resiliency programmes where a failover posture for critical interfaces must be maintained continuously; peak events where failover needs to be pre-staged for traffic surges; and compliance and audit requirements where evidence of DR readiness and traceable execution history is needed.

 

iFlow AI Advisory App : Design Intelligence for Dev Ease

The iFlow AI Advisory App addresses a challenge that emerges as integration teams start developing new flows and extending migrated ones on SAP Integration Suite: without deep platform expertise, developers default to familiar patterns from legacy platforms that are not optimal on BTP.

The iFlow AI Advisory App provides automated design recommendations, guiding integration teams to the most appropriate iFlow patterns for each use case. Rather than waiting for an architecture review to flag suboptimal designs, the advisory capability operates at development time, preventing technical debt from accumulating in the new landscape before it has even started.

This capability is particularly valuable for organisations that are extending their SAP Integration Suite footprint beyond the migrated interfaces into net-new integration development, ensuring that the same automation-first, pattern-led approach that characterised the migration carries through into ongoing development.

 

Transport Management & Governance Tool

Governance of the integration landscape, managing the lifecycle of iFlows from development through QA to production, is a critical operational capability that is easy to underestimate. Without structured transport management, teams resort to manual deployment procedures, version confusion, and the risk of deploying untested changes to production.

The iVolve Transport Management and Governance Tool provides the operational rigour that enterprise integration landscapes require:

  • Full transport management capabilities with controlled promotion from development through QA to production
  • Artefact comparison and version management -> compare versions before deployment to understand exactly what is changing
  • Easy access to adapter configuration details across the landscape
  • Externalised parameter management for environment-specific configurations

 

Enhanced Monitoring and AI-Powered Operations

SAP Integration Suite's native monitoring provides a solid baseline, but enterprise operations teams consistently find they need capabilities that go further particularly around error management, reporting, and proactive intelligence.

Enhanced Monitoring Tool

Built on SAP standards for seamless integration, the Enhanced Monitoring Tool provides full monitoring reports with downloadable error outputs for efficient debugging. Its restart functionality and searchable interface  by sender, receiver, message type, application ID, date, time, and statistical aggregates, enables operations teams to move from reactive firefighting to proactive management.

AI-Powered Alert Notifications

iVolve's AI-enhanced notification capability leverages SAP BTP's Integration Suite AI Adapter to enrich alert emails with GenAI-generated recommendations, giving operations teams not just the notification of a failure, but context-aware guidance on likely causes and resolution steps. This is the beginning of a shift from monitoring to intelligence-assisted operations.

Periodic Monitoring AI Agent

Looking ahead to the 2026 roadmap, Tarento is building out an autonomous Periodic Monitoring Agent that will automate routine manual monitoring activities using the iVolve AI platform, removing the operational overhead of scheduled checks and exception reviews from the integration operations team entirely.

 

The OpEase Suite as a Competitive Advantage

 Organisations that deploy the full OpEase Suite alongside their SAP Integration Suite migration achieve something qualitatively different from those that treat DevOps as an afterthought. They have a resilient, governed, intelligence-enhanced integration operations model from day one of go-live. Their operations teams are not firefighting  but are managing by exception, with tooling that surfaces the information they need and automates the responses they would otherwise need to execute manually.

 

 

Bringing It All Together: The Full iVolve Journey

Across this series, we have traced the complete arc of integration modernisation with iVolve; from the strategic imperative and business case, through automated discovery, migration and testing, to the operational excellence of the OpEase Suite. In the next and final blog of this series, we will deep dive into OneFailOver Application and how it helps enterprises architect and manage high-resiliency, and take control over the HA/Failover process in their organisations.

Across the series, the consistent thread has been automation: reducing the manual effort, the human error, and the programme risk that have historically made integration modernisation programmes difficult to justify and difficult to deliver.

Tarento’s iVolve programme is the product of deep SAP integration expertise, proprietary tooling built and refined over dozens of programmes, and a genuine commitment to delivering the ROI that customers calculate before they commit. 

 

Take the Next Step: iVolve is available on the SAP Store. Tarento is an SAP Validated Partner Use Case (VPUC) holder and SAP Migration Factory Partner. To start a Discovery engagement or run an ROI analysis for your landscape, drop me a note on shabarish.nair@tarento.com.

entry.title: iVolve Series | Part E : When Downtime Is Not an Option
entry.content:
Note: This blog is part of The iVolve Series | Your Integration Estate Is Holding You Back. Well, It Doesn’t Have To. Read the previous blog in this series, iVolve Series | Part D : Beyond Go-Live before continuing.

It is 11:47 PM. An online retailer’s Black Friday weekend promotion has just gone live. Orders are flooding in at three times the normal rate. Every order triggers a cascade of integration events; stock reservation, payment confirmation, warehouse dispatch, carrier booking, customer notification. All of this runs through SAP Integration Suite. And at 11:52 PM, comes an announcement of an emergency platform maintenance window. The primary tenant will be unavailable for the next 45 minutes.

For organisations without a failover strategy, this is a crisis. For organisations running OneFailover, it is a non-event. Traffic is rerouted to the failover tenant in seconds. The integration landscape keeps running. And the end customers never notice. Seamless as seamless can be!

This blog examines why integration resilience is a board-level concern for any enterprise that relies on SAP Integration Suite for real-time business operations and how Tarento’s OneFailover application transforms a complex, manual, high-risk process into a repeatable, automated, and auditable programme capability.

 

The Integration Layer Is the Business

For a previous generation of enterprises, integration middleware was infrastructure; important, but not business-critical in the way that customer-facing systems were. That distinction has collapsed. For any organisation that has consolidated its integration estate onto SAP Integration Suite, the platform is now the nervous system of real-time business operations.

Consider the industries where this is most acute. A large e-commerce retailer processes tens of thousands of orders per hour during peak periods; every order is an integration event. A global manufacturer runs real-time production planning and supplier collaboration through integration flows; a 30-minute outage ripples into production delays, penalty clauses, and customer escalations. A financial services firm processes payment instructions and regulatory reporting through integration; downtime is a compliance exposure, not just an operational inconvenience. A healthcare provider coordinates patient data flows, bed management, and pharmacy dispensing through its integration platform; unavailability can directly affect patient outcomes.

For all of these organisations, the question of what happens when SAP Integration Suite is unavailable - whether for a planned maintenance window, a regional incident, or a capacity event is not a theoretical IT risk. It is a live business continuity question that demands a credible, tested answer.

 

The Downtime Problem Is More Complex Than It Looks

SAP Integration Suite is a managed cloud service. This brings enormous benefits - Continuous updates, elastic scaling, no infrastructure management overhead. But it also means that maintenance is scheduled by SAP, not by the enterprise. Planned maintenance windows are notified in advance but are not negotiable. And unplanned incidents, while rare on a well-operated cloud platform, cannot be eliminated entirely.

The naïve response is to simply provision a second tenant and manually copy everything across when needed. In practice, this approach fails in three ways.

First, the synchronisation problem: a large integration landscape may contain hundreds of iFlows, message mappings, value mappings, scripts, security artefacts, and configuration parameters. Keeping a secondary tenant in sync with all of these manually is a significant ongoing operational burden, one that is frequently deprioritised until it is too late.

Second, the execution problem: when an incident occurs or a maintenance window opens, switching traffic to a secondary tenant requires precise, sequenced steps across multiple systems - DNS or traffic manager changes, iFlow activation, listener cutover, and verification. Doing this under pressure, manually, from a runbook, at 2 AM, creates unacceptable risk of human error.

Third, the governance problem: many regulated industries require documented, auditable evidence of disaster recovery capability. A manual failover process that lives in a shared document and has never been tested in a controlled way does not satisfy this requirement.

 

The Three Failure Modes of Manual Failover

Synchronisation drift means the failover tenant is never truly ready. Execution risk means the switchover itself is the most dangerous moment. Governance gaps mean the organisation cannot demonstrate readiness to auditors or regulators. OneFailover addresses all three simultaneously.

 

 

Introducing OneFailover: An Operational Layer for Integration Resilience

OneFailover is Tarento’s purpose-built application for SAP Integration Suite tenant resilience — available on the SAP Store and used by enterprises running large, mission-critical integration landscapes. It was formerly known as Unified Failover Management and has evolved into a comprehensive operational platform that addresses every dimension of the failover challenge: continuous synchronisation, controlled cutover, backup and recovery, observability, and governance.

The core design principle is that failover should never be an emergency response. It should be a routine, well-rehearsed capability that an operations team can invoke with confidence, whether for a scheduled maintenance window, a regional resiliency exercise, or an actual incident. OneFailover is built around that principle from the ground up.

 

The Six Pillars of OneFailover

 Pillar 1: Continuous Sync and Orchestration

OneFailover maintains a full-scope, continuously synchronised replica of the primary tenant across all artefact types: iFlows, Message Mappings, Value Mappings, and Scripts. Synchronisation is dependency-aware, artefacts that depend on each other are always moved together, in the correct sequence, preventing partial deployments that would cause failures. Group and sub-group priorities allow integration teams to define which business domains and interface clusters are synchronised first, ensuring the most critical processes are always in the highest state of readiness on the failover tenant. Single-side or both-side deployment options give teams flexibility in how they manage the synchronisation cadence.

Pillar 2: Failover Control and Cutover Execution

When the moment of cutover arrives, planned or unplanned, OneFailover provides deterministic, orchestrated execution. The group-based methodology ensures that interface clusters are activated on the failover tenant in the correct business-aware sequence: order management before warehouse execution, payment before fulfilment. OneFailover Profiles capture the complete primary-to-failover mapping configuration, including Git targets, so that the same cutover procedure can be repeated identically every time - in a drill, in a maintenance window, or in a genuine incident. An admin lock prevents simultaneous actions by multiple operators during a cutover, eliminating the risk of conflicting changes in a high-pressure situation. Manual listener cutover ensures that human confirmation is required at the point of traffic switching, maintaining control at the most consequential moment.

The cutover integrates with enterprise routing infrastructure. For inbound traffic switching, OneFailover works with Azure Traffic Manager in a priority-based setup, or with internal DNS solutions such as F5 or Citrix NetScaler, fitting into the network architecture that the enterprise already operates rather than requiring new infrastructure to be introduced.

Pillar 3: Backup, Restore and Version Control

OneFailover provides GitHub-based, artefact-level versioning for the integration landscape. Every iFlow, mapping, and configuration is versioned individually, enabling selective restore with overwrite guardrails that prevent accidental rollbacks. Failover configurations, the OneFailover Profiles, can be exported and imported, enabling them to be maintained, version-controlled, and shared across teams. This creates a complete audit trail of the integration landscape’s state over time, supporting change management, incident investigation, and regulatory compliance requirements.

Pillar 4: Multi-Runtime and Multi-Tenant Awareness

Large enterprise integration landscapes are not homogeneous. They typically span multiple SAP Integration Suite tenants, multiple runtimes including Cloud Foundry and Edge Integration Cells for sensitive on-premise data, and multiple business domains with different criticality levels and ownership. OneFailover is designed for this complexity. Its persistence layer maintains a complete inventory of iFlows and tenant metadata across the full landscape, providing a single point of control and visibility regardless of how many tenants and runtimes are in scope. Edge Cell awareness means that even hybrid landscapes  with interfaces running on-premise for data sovereignty or latency reasons, are fully included in the failover posture.

Pillar 5: Observability and Operational Dashboards

Knowing that a failover tenant exists is not the same as knowing that it is ready. OneFailover provides continuous Readiness Scores for each tenant and runtime, a quantified measure of how current the failover environment is relative to the primary, how recently it was tested, and whether any synchronisation gaps or configuration discrepancies exist. Operational Status and Monitoring dashboards give integration operations teams real-time visibility into the state of both primary and failover environments, with activity and execution logs that provide a complete record of every action taken. Throughput optimisations ensure that synchronisation activity does not create performance overhead on the primary tenant during business hours.

Pillar 6: Security, Compliance and Secret Safety

Integration landscapes contain sensitive security artefacts: User Credentials, Global Variables, Number Ranges, and Access Policies. OneFailover includes all of these in its synchronisation and backup scope, ensuring that the failover tenant is not just functionally complete but operationally ready to process real transactions. Security is maintained through SAP BTP Identity Authentication Service-powered authentication and strict secret-safe metadata handling: credentials and sensitive values are referenced by hash or reference only, never stored in plain text in version control. Strong guardrails prevent accidental exposure of production credentials during failover operations.

 

Why SAP’s Standard Capabilities Are Not Enough: The Case for OneFailover

A common assumption when enterprises first deploy SAP Integration Suite is that high availability and disaster recovery are simply included, that because SAP is a managed cloud service, the resilience problem is solved by default. This assumption is partially correct and significantly incomplete. SAP Integration Suite does provide genuine, well-engineered high availability within a single region through multi-availability zone redundancy.

For most routine operational scenarios, the above is sufficient. But for enterprises that require cross-regional disaster recovery, planned maintenance execution without service interruption, automated failover with deterministic cutover sequences, and the kind of audit-ready, governance-controlled resilience posture that regulated industries must demonstrate, SAP’s native capabilities leave substantial gaps that OneFailover is specifically engineered to fill.

The table below maps each dimension of enterprise integration resilience against what SAP provides natively and what OneFailover adds, making explicit where the standard platform ends and where OneFailover begins.

The pattern across every dimension in this table is consistent: SAP provides the foundation, and OneFailover provides the operational layer that transforms that foundation into a fully governed, automated, and enterprise-grade resilience posture. These are capabilities that matter most when a regional incident, a regulatory audit, or a peak trading event puts real pressure on the integration landscape. Treating them as optional is, in effect, a decision to accept that risk rather than manage it.

For integration teams making the case internally for OneFailover investment, this table is often the most effective starting point: it places SAP’s own standard offering alongside OneFailover’s capabilities, making the gap visible and the value proposition concrete across every dimension that leadership, operations, and compliance stakeholders care about.

 

Planning for the Four Scenarios That Matter

OneFailover’s capabilities map directly onto the four operational scenarios where integration resilience is genuinely tested:

Scenario 1: Planned Maintenance Windows

SAP notifies maintenance windows in advance, giving integration teams time to prepare. With OneFailover, preparation is straightforward: verify the Readiness Score, run a pre-cutover sync to ensure the failover tenant reflects all recent changes, and execute the controlled cutover through the OneFailover portal. The maintenance window passes with no business disruption. When the primary tenant is restored, failback follows the same orchestrated process in reverse. What would otherwise require a team of engineers working through a complex runbook becomes a two-person operation managed through an intuitive interface.

Scenario 2: Regional Resiliency and Unplanned Incidents

For organisations with a global integration landscape, regional incidents like a cloud availability zone event, a network disruption, a regional regulatory action etc may require rerouting integration traffic to a different data centre or availability zone. The risk calculus has also shifted. Recent geopolitical conflicts most notably the US/Israel and Iran confrontation have seen data centres targeted as part of military operations, causing prolonged regional outages that no amount of within-region redundancy could mitigate. Cloud infrastructure is not insulated from the consequences of armed conflict, and organisations with dependencies in or routing through affected regions are finding that out in real time. Regional resilience is no longer a contingency for rare technical failures it is a standing operational requirement. 

OneFailover’s multi-tenant and multi-runtime awareness, combined with its integration with Azure Traffic Manager and enterprise DNS infrastructure, makes regional rerouting a controlled operation rather than a crisis response. The failover tenant in a secondary region is continuously maintained in a ready state so that when rerouting is needed, whether triggered by a scheduled maintenance window or an unplanned regional event, the tenant is already current and the cutover can be executed without scrambling to synchronise artefacts under pressure.

Scenario 3: Peak Traffic Events

For e-commerce retailers, peak trading periods i.e. Black Friday, Cyber Monday, end-of-season sales etc represent the highest-value and highest-risk windows of the year. An integration outage during a 48-hour peak event can represent millions in lost revenue and lasting reputational damage. OneFailover provides the assurance that integration teams need to approach peak periods with confidence: a fully synchronised, tested, and ready failover tenant that can absorb traffic within seconds if the primary environment comes under strain or experiences an incident. The Readiness Score dashboard gives leadership a quantified, real-time view of DR preparedness in the days and hours before a peak event goes live.

Scenario 4: Compliance, Audit and DR Certification

Regulated industries; financial services, healthcare, utilities, public sector are increasingly required to demonstrate integration resilience as part of broader IT operational resilience or business continuity frameworks. The EU’s Digital Operational Resilience Act (DORA), for example, requires financial entities to test their ICT continuity measures, maintain documented recovery procedures, and demonstrate that critical third-party dependencies, including cloud platforms are covered by their resilience programmes.

OneFailover’s activity logs, execution records, Readiness Scores, and repeatable playbooks provide exactly the evidence base that internal audit, external auditors, and regulators need to see: documented DR capability, tested regularly, with traceable execution history.

 

What Enterprises Gain: The Business Value Equation

 OneFailover delivers value across four dimensions that map directly to business outcomes rather than IT metrics:

  • Lower operational risk: integration outages during maintenance windows, peak events, or incidents are eliminated as a source of business disruption. The risk is not mitigated but is engineered out of the operating model
  • Faster, repeatable failover: what previously took hours of manual coordination is reduced to minutes of portal-guided execution. The same process runs identically every time, removing the dependency on tribal knowledge and individual expertise
  • Improved governance: every action is logged, every artefact is versioned, and every failover execution is traceable. The integration landscape becomes auditable in a way it never was with legacy middleware or manual processes
  • Higher confidence: integration teams, operations leadership, and the business can approach maintenance windows and peak events knowing that the failover posture is quantified, tested, and ready

 

 The 2026 Roadmap: Making OneFailover Even Smarter

Tarento’s 2026 roadmap for OneFailover extends the platform into several high-value adjacent areas. API Management integration will bring API policies and configurations into the sync and failover scope, ensuring that API-fronted integrations maintain consistent behaviour across primary and failover environments. A Centralised Key Vault integration will further strengthen the security model for credential and secret management across tenants. Advanced Proactive Alerting will shift OneFailover from a reactive tool into a predictive one, notifying operations teams of synchronisation drift or readiness degradation before it becomes a risk. Trading Partner Management capabilities will extend the failover posture to cover B2B integration configurations, ensuring that EDI and partner-facing integrations are included in the resilience framework alongside internal A2A flows.

 

Integration Resilience as a Programme Capability, Not an Afterthought

The organisations that get the most from SAP Integration Suite are those that treat it as a strategic platform, not just infrastructure. That means investing not only in migration quality and development standards, but in the operational model that keeps the platform running at the level of reliability the business requires.

For any enterprise where integration is the backbone of real-time business operations, whether in retail, manufacturing, financial services, healthcare, or logistics - OneFailover is not an optional enhancement to a well-functioning SAP Integration Suite deployment. It is the mechanism that makes a well-functioning deployment genuinely enterprise-ready: predictable, resilient, governed, and prepared for the scenarios that matter most.

The scenario at the start of this blog; an e-retailer navigating a Friday night urgent maintenance window at peak trading, illustrates what is at stake. With OneFailover, the answer to that scenario is simple: the integration landscape keeps running. The customers keep doing what they do. The business keeps moving. And when the primary tenant comes back online, failback runs just as cleanly as failover did.

 

Get Started: To discuss how OneFailover fits into your SAP Integration Suite operating model, and exploring architecting a 'high availability, high resiliency' integration landscape, drop me a note on shabarish.nair@tarento.com.

entry.title: SAP Cloud Integration (CI/CPI) Dynamic Custom Headers for Monitoring - Config Driven Approach
entry.content:
When working with SAP Cloud Integration (CI/CPI), one of the common questions from operations teams is:

“How can we quickly add a new custom header to Message Monitoring without transporting the changes of iFlow?”

Maybe you need to filter messages by Delivery Number, Shipping Point, Sales Order, or something else that wasn’t planned during development.
With the standard approach, you have to modify the iFlow, add a custom header  property, transport the change through the landscape, and redeploy. This takes time and coordination.

The approach described in this post solves that problem by externalizing the monitoring rules in a governed JSON configuration, while the extraction logic remains in a reusable Script Collection.
This lets you add new monitoring fields at runtime, in a controlled and maintainable way.

What this solution enables?

  • Add/remove monitoring fields without updating each iFlow
  • Fully governed: all rules are controlled via a single JSON config
  • Centralized script in a Script Collection (no duplication)
  • Supports both XML and JSON payloads
  • Multiple extraction styles supported (XPath, deep search, JSONPath-lite)
  • Values appear in Message Monitoring as Custom Header Properties, which can be searched and filtered

    SAP’s documentation confirms that MPL custom header properties can be written via scripting, which makes them available for searching in the monitor.

    (Reference: https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-integration/sap-cloud-integration/use-custom-header-properties-to-search-for-message-processing-logs?locale=en-US)

    SAP also recommends using Script Collections for reuse, central maintenance, and to avoid duplicating scripts across integration flows.

    (Reference: https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-integration/sap-cloud-integration/developing-script-and-script-collection?locale=en-US&q=Script+Collection)

    Architecture Overview:

 

The design has three simple moving parts:

1) Global JSON Configuration (Governed)

A common iflow is used to write a global variable with global scope. The value(externalized) is a JSON document which defines custom header properties needed for respective iFlows to be monitored in the message monitoring.

Example JSON configuration:

{
  "root": {
    "Iflow": [
      {
        "IflowID": "OutboundDelivery_-_Sample",
        "HeaderDetails": [
          {
            "HeaderName": "DeliveryNumber",
            "HeaderPath": "DeliveryNumber"
          },
          {
            "HeaderName": "ShippingPoint",
            "HeaderPath": "//ShippingPoint"
          }  
        ]
      },
       {
        "IflowID": "SalesOrder_-_Sample",
        "HeaderDetails": [
          {
            "HeaderName": "SalesOrderNumber",
            "HeaderPath": "SalesOrderNumber"
          }  
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}

 

You can easily add more fields or more iFlows to this JSON without touching any of the iFlows if new custom header properties are needed.

2) iFlow Property: HeaderConfigJson

Each iFlow loads the JSON config into an exchange property called HeaderConfigJson. Here, OutboundDelivery - Sample is an iFlow in which I want to add CustomHeaderProperties to monitor them in message monitoring.

3) Reusable Script Collection

A Groovy script (dynamicCustomHeaders.groovy) stored in a Script Collection reads the config, extracts values from the payload, and writes them into MPL custom header properties.

This way, each iFlow remains clean: it only needs one property assignment + one Script step.

Step-by-Step Implementation

Step 1: Create a Script Collection

  1. Go to your Integration Package
  2. Add a Script Collection artifact
  3. Upload the reusable script dynamicCustomHeaders.groovy
  4. Deploy the Script Collection
import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.json.JsonSlurper

import org.apache.camel.Exchange
import org.apache.camel.builder.SimpleBuilder

import javax.xml.parsers.DocumentBuilderFactory
import javax.xml.xpath.XPathFactory
import javax.xml.xpath.XPathConstants
import javax.xml.namespace.NamespaceContext

import org.w3c.dom.NodeList

Message processData(Message message) {

    // --- iFlow runtime id (camelId) -> stored in MPL as custom header property
    Exchange exchange = message.exchange
    String runtimeId = SimpleBuilder.simple('${camelId}')
            .evaluate(exchange, String)
            ?.toString()
            ?.trim()

    def messageLog = messageLogFactory?.getMessageLog(message)
    if (messageLog != null && runtimeId) {
        messageLog.addCustomHeaderProperty('IFlowId', runtimeId)
    }

    // --- Read config JSON from exchange property
    String cfgText = message.getProperty("HeaderConfigJson") as String
    if (!cfgText || cfgText.trim().isEmpty()) {
        return message
    }

    def cfg
    try {
        cfg = new JsonSlurper().parse(new StringReader(cfgText))
    } catch (Exception e) {
        // Invalid config -> do nothing in production (or throw if you prefer)
        return message
    }

    def iflowList = cfg?.root?.Iflow
    if (!iflowList) return message
    if (!(iflowList instanceof List)) iflowList = [iflowList]

    def selectedIflow = iflowList.find { it?.IflowID?.toString()?.trim()?.equalsIgnoreCase(runtimeId) }
    if (!selectedIflow) selectedIflow = iflowList[0]

    def headerDetails = selectedIflow?.HeaderDetails
    if (!headerDetails) return message
    if (!(headerDetails instanceof List)) headerDetails = [headerDetails]

    // --- Detect payload type
    String payload = message.getBody(String) as String
    if (!payload || payload.trim().isEmpty()) return message
    String p = payload.trim()

    boolean isXml  = p.startsWith("<")
    boolean isJson = p.startsWith("{") || p.startsWith("[")

    if (!isXml && !isJson) return message

    // ---------------- JSON helpers (used only if JSON payload) ----------------
    def deepFindValuesByKey
    deepFindValuesByKey = { Object obj, String key, List acc ->
        if (obj instanceof Map) {
            obj.each { k, v ->
                if (k?.toString() == key) acc << v
                deepFindValuesByKey(v, key, acc)
            }
        } else if (obj instanceof List) {
            obj.each { item -> deepFindValuesByKey(item, key, acc) }
        }
        return acc
    }

    def evalJsonPath = { Object root, String path ->
        if (!path) return []

        // Deep search: //KeyName
        if (path.startsWith("//")) {
            String key = path.substring(2)
            def found = deepFindValuesByKey(root, key, [])
            def flat = []
            found.each { v ->
                if (v instanceof List) flat.addAll(v)
                else flat << v
            }
            return flat
        }

        // JSONPath-lite: $.a.b[0].c or $.a[*].b
        if (!path.startsWith("\$.")) return []

        def tokens = path.substring(2).split("\\.")
        def current = [root]

        tokens.each { t ->
            def next = []
            def mIndex = (t =~ /^([^\[]+)\[(\d+|\*)\]$/)

            if (mIndex.matches()) {
                String name = mIndex[0][1]
                String idx  = mIndex[0][2]

                current.each { c ->
                    def node = (c instanceof Map) ? c[name] : null
                    if (node instanceof List) {
                        if (idx == "*") {
                            next.addAll(node)
                        } else {
                            int i = Integer.parseInt(idx)
                            if (i >= 0 && i < node.size()) next << node[i]
                        }
                    }
                }
            } else {
                current.each { c ->
                    if (c instanceof Map && c.containsKey(t)) {
                        next << c[t]
                    } else if (c instanceof List) {
                        c.each { item ->
                            if (item instanceof Map && item.containsKey(t)) next << item[t]
                        }
                    }
                }
            }

            current = next
        }

        def out = []
        current.each { v ->
            if (v instanceof List) out.addAll(v)
            else out << v
        }
        return out
    }

    // ---------------- XML setup (used only if XML payload) ----------------
    def doc = null
    def xPath = null
    String rootNsUri = ""

    def toLocalNameXPath = { String plainXPath ->
        if (!plainXPath) return plainXPath

        // //TagName -> //*[local-name()='TagName']
        if (plainXPath.startsWith("//")) {
            String tail = plainXPath.substring(2)
            if (tail && !tail.contains("/") && !tail.contains(":") && !tail.contains("(") && !tail.contains("@")) {
                return "//*[local-name()='" + tail + "']"
            }
        }

        // TagName -> //*[local-name()='TagName']
        if (!plainXPath.startsWith("/") && !plainXPath.startsWith("//")) {
            return "//*[local-name()='" + plainXPath + "']"
        }

        // /A/B[1]/C -> /*[local-name()='A']/*[local-name()='B'][1]/*[local-name()='C']
        def parts = plainXPath.tokenize('/')
        def sb = new StringBuilder()

        parts.each { part ->
            if (!part) return

            if (part.startsWith("@") || part.contains("(") || part == "." || part == ".." || part == "*") {
                sb.append("/").append(part)
                return
            }

            def m = (part =~ /^([^\[]+)(\[\d+\])?$/)
            if (m.matches()) {
                def qname = m[0][1]
                def idx   = m[0][2] ?: ""
                def local = qname.contains(":") ? qname.substring(qname.indexOf(":") + 1) : qname
                sb.append("/*[local-name()='").append(local).append("']").append(idx)
            } else {
                sb = new StringBuilder(plainXPath)
            }
        }

        return sb.toString()
    }

    Object jsonObj = null
    if (isJson) {
        try {
            jsonObj = new JsonSlurper().parseText(p)
        } catch (Exception e) {
            return message
        }
    }

    if (isXml) {
        try {
            def dbf = DocumentBuilderFactory.newInstance()
            dbf.setNamespaceAware(true)
            doc = dbf.newDocumentBuilder().parse(new ByteArrayInputStream(p.getBytes("UTF-8")))

            rootNsUri = doc?.documentElement?.namespaceURI ?: ""
            xPath = XPathFactory.newInstance().newXPath()

            xPath.setNamespaceContext(new NamespaceContext() {
                String getNamespaceURI(String prefix) {
                    if (prefix == null) return ""
                    if ("xml".equals(prefix)) return "http://www.w3.org/XML/1998/namespace"

                    String uri = doc.lookupNamespaceURI(prefix)
                    if (uri != null && uri.length() > 0) return uri

                    if (prefix.length() == 0) {
                        String defUri = doc.lookupNamespaceURI(null)
                        return defUri != null ? defUri : ""
                    }

                    return rootNsUri != null ? rootNsUri : ""
                }
                String getPrefix(String namespaceURI) { return null }
                Iterator getPrefixes(String namespaceURI) { return null }
            })
        } catch (Exception e) {
            return message
        }
    }

    // ---------------- Apply extraction rules ----------------
    headerDetails.each { hd ->
        String headerName = hd?.HeaderName?.toString()
        def headerPathRaw = hd?.HeaderPath

        if (!headerName || headerPathRaw == null) return

        String headerPath = headerPathRaw.toString().trim()
        if (!headerPath) return

        def values = []

        if (isXml && doc != null && xPath != null) {
            String effectiveXpath = headerPath

            // If XML is namespaced and XPath has no prefix, rewrite to local-name()
            if (rootNsUri && !headerPath.contains(":")) {
                effectiveXpath = toLocalNameXPath(headerPath)
            } else if (!headerPath.startsWith("/") && !headerPath.startsWith("//")) {
                // non-namespaced XML + simple name
                effectiveXpath = "//*[local-name()='" + headerPath + "']"
            }

            try {
                NodeList nodes = (NodeList) xPath.evaluate(effectiveXpath, doc, XPathConstants.NODESET)
                for (int i = 0; i < nodes.length; i++) {
                    def v = nodes.item(i)?.getTextContent()
                    if (v != null) {
                        v = v.toString().trim()
                        if (v) values << v
                    }
                }
            } catch (Exception ignore) {
                // skip
            }
        }

        if (isJson && jsonObj != null) {
            def found = []
            try {
                if (headerPath.startsWith("//") || headerPath.startsWith("\$.")) {
                    found = evalJsonPath(jsonObj, headerPath)
                } else {
                    found = deepFindValuesByKey(jsonObj, headerPath, [])
                }
            } catch (Exception ignore) {
                found = []
            }

            found.each { v ->
                if (v == null) return
                String s = v.toString().trim()
                if (s) values << s
            }
        }

        String finalValue = values.join(",")

        // Set runtime header always (empty string is okay)
        message.setHeader(headerName, finalValue)

        // Write to MPL only if value exists (keeps Monitoring clean)
        if (messageLog != null && finalValue) {
            messageLog.addCustomHeaderProperty(headerName, finalValue)
        }
    }

    return message
}

Step 2: Store JSON config as a Global Variable

Place your JSON global variable.
This becomes the governed source of truth for all monitoring headers.

{
  "root": {
    "Iflow": [
      {
        "IflowID": "OutboundDelivery_-_Sample",
        "HeaderDetails": [
          {
            "HeaderName": "DeliveryNumber",
            "HeaderPath": "DeliveryNumber"
          },
          {
            "HeaderName": "ShippingPoint",
            "HeaderPath": "//ShippingPoint"
          }  
        ]
      },
       {
        "IflowID": "SalesOrder_-_Sample",
        "HeaderDetails": [
          {
            "HeaderName": "SalesOrderNumber",
            "HeaderPath": "SalesOrderNumber"
          }  
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}

Step 3: Reference JSON in iFlow

Inside the iFlow (OutboundDelivery – Sample):

  • Add a Content Modifier  step
  • Create exchange property:
    • Name: HeaderConfigJson
    • Value: ${globalVariable.GlobalCustomHeaderConfig} (your variable)

Step 4: Add Script Step (Reusable)

Add a Script step and select the script from the Script Collection.

This keeps your iFlow lightweight and centralized.

How it works behind the scenes

1. Identifying the running iFlow

To select the right config, the script reads the runtime iFlow ID using:

SimpleBuilder.simple('${camelId}').evaluate(exchange, String)
 
2. Detecting the payload type (XML vs JSON)

The script inspects the first character:

  • < → XML
  • { or [ → JSON

3. Extracting values

For XML:

  • Parses using DocumentBuilderFactory with namespaceAware = true
  • Supports multiple HeaderPath formats:
    • //DeliveryNumber (deep search)
    • DeliveryNumber (shorthand)
    • Absolute XPath /OutboundDelivery/...
  • Handles namespaces via the local-name() technique when needed
    (XPath is namespace-sensitive; unprefixed paths normally do not match namespaced XML.)

For JSON:

Supports:

  • Deep search (//DeliveryNumber)
  • Shorthand (DeliveryNumber)
  • JSONPath-lite ($.OutboundDelivery.DeliveryHeader.DeliveryNumber)
  • Matching multiple values and joining them

4. Writing to Monitoring

Values are written using:

messageLog.addCustomHeaderProperty(...)
 
What you see in Message Monitoring

After message processing:

Here you’ll see the extracted values (DeliveryNumber, SalesOrderNumber, etc.), which you can use to filter and search messages.

Example with XML (Namespaced)

Payload:

 
<od:OutboundDelivery xmlns:od="urn:example:outbounddelivery:v1">
<od:DeliveryHeader>
<od:DeliveryNumber>80012346</od:DeliveryNumber>
<od:ShippingPoint>0001</od:ShippingPoint>
</od:DeliveryHeader>
</od:OutboundDelivery>
 
Config:
 
{ "HeaderName": "DeliveryNumber", "HeaderPath": "//DeliveryNumber" }
{ "HeaderName": "ShippingPoint", "HeaderPath": "//ShippingPoint" }

Result in monitor:

  • DeliveryNumber = 80012346
  • ShippingPoint = 0001

The script resolves namespaces automatically using prefix-independent matching.

Different Ways to Configure HeaderPath for XML & JSON Inputs:

Payload Type Example Payload Config Example (HeaderPath) How Script Interprets It Expected Monitoring Result
XML (no namespace)<DeliveryNumber>80012345</DeliveryNumber>"//DeliveryNumber"Deep XML search (prefix‑agnostic)DeliveryNumber = 80012345
XML (no namespace)Same as Case 1"DeliveryNumber"Shorthand → auto‑converted to //*[local-name()='DeliveryNumber']DeliveryNumber = 80012345
XML (no namespace)Same"/OutboundDelivery/DeliveryHeader[1]/DeliveryNumber[1]"Absolute XPath; used directlyDeliveryNumber = 80012345
XML (namespaced, prefix = od)**<od:DeliveryNumber>80012346</od:DeliveryNumber>"//DeliveryNumber"Auto‑converted to namespace‑safe XPath using local-name() (because XPath without prefix does not match namespaced XML)DeliveryNumber = 80012346
XML (namespaced, prefix = od)**Same"//od:DeliveryNumber"Script resolves prefix → namespace URI dynamicallyDeliveryNumber = 80012346
XML (namespaced, prefix = ns1)**<ns1:DeliveryNumber> but config uses "//od:DeliveryNumber""//od:DeliveryNumber"Script remaps any prefix to the correct URI based on XML (prefix‑agnostic)DeliveryNumber = value
JSON"DeliveryNumber": "80012345""//DeliveryNumber"Deep JSON search by key name anywhere in the treeDeliveryNumber = 80012345
JSONSame"DeliveryNumber"Shorthand key name → deep searchDeliveryNumber = 80012345
JSONItems array"//ItemNumber"Deep search → collects all occurrencesAllItemNumbers = 000010,000020
JSONSame"$.OutboundDelivery.DeliveryHeader.DeliveryNumber"JSONPath‑lite → exact extractionDeliveryNumber = 80012345
JSON array"DeliveryItems": [{ItemNumber}]"$.OutboundDelivery.DeliveryItems[*].ItemNumber"JSONPath‑lite with wildcard → collect all itemsAllItemNumbers = 000010,000020
JSON + XML mixed iFlowsAnyAny of the aboveScript automatically detects payload type (< vs { or [), then applies appropriate extraction logicAlways extracts correct values

Governance Recommendations:

  • Manage the JSON config as a controlled operational artifact
  • Keep header names business-friendly
  • Put validation rules or whitelists if needed
  • Periodically review which monitoring fields provide actual value

Troubleshooting:

Custom header not showing in MPL?

Ensure you use addCustomHeaderProperty (message headers alone won’t show up).

Value empty for XML?

Namespace mismatch. XPath matching is namespace-sensitive; prefix-independent matching fixes this.

Wrong config?

Double-check IflowID vs ${camelId} mapping (SimpleBuilder evaluation).

Final Thoughts:

This pattern gives both development and operations teams a powerful, flexible, and maintainable way to enhance monitoring without repeatedly modifying or transporting iFlows.

  • Configurable via Global Variables
  • Reusable via Script Collection
  • Monitoring enhanced using MPL Custom Header Properties
  • Supports XML + JSON
  • Safe, governed, and easy to extend

entry.title: End-to-End Mail Adapter Setup in SAP CPI Using Gmail (Step-by-Step Guide)
entry.content:
Introduction :  This article provides a comprehensive end-to-end guide to configuring the Mail Receiver Adapter in SAP Cloud Platform Integration (CPI) using a personal Gmail account. It explains the complete setup process, including the required Gmail configuration, security settings, and the detailed steps to configure the Mail Receiver Adapter within an integration flow. 

  • Enabling 2-Step Verification in Gmail

  • Generating a secure App Password

  • Creating User Credentials in CPI Security Material

Step 1 : Setting up the app password in Gmail.

To set up the app password 

  • Log in to your Gmail account.

  • Go to Google Account → Security.

  • Enable 2-Step Verification (mandatory).

  • After enabling 2-Step Verification, you will see App passwords.

  • Create a new App Password:

    • App: Mail

    • Device: Other (Custom name – SAP CPI)

  • Google will generate a 16-character password.

  • Use this password in CPI (without spaces).

Note : App Password works only if 2-Step Verification is enabled.

After creation you'll get a password which is generated 

Step 2 : Now we have  to store these credentials in cpi security material.

  • Go to Monitor → Manage Security → Security Material

  • Create User Credentials

  • Name: (e.g., gmailcredentials)

  • Username: Full Gmail address

  • Password: App Password (not Gmail login password)

The name which we have given here will be used in within the I flow

And the user should be  our mail ID  and in password we have give the app password which is generated.

Step 3 : Connectivity Test (certificate upload)

Now we have to test the connectivity towards the Gmail in Test connectivity tile.

  • Go to Monitor → Integrations & APIs → Connectivity Tests

  • Choose SMTP

  • Provide:

    • Host: smtp.gmail.com

    • Port: 587

    • Protection: STARTTLS

    • Authentication: Plain User/Password

    • Credential Name: gmailcredentials

Keystore: Upload the downloaded certificates here.

Step 4 : Create Integration Flow.

Here I have taken a simple content modifier with a body so that the message in content modifier will be triggered to our inbox

The body : 

 

Mail adapter : The To address should be same as what we have deployed in security material user credentials.

Step 5 : Testing 

In postman give the credential details and test it you'll getting the response and the mail will be triggered to your Gmail as well

We'll get a mail as below.

Summary : This blog explains how to configure and connect the SAP CPI Mail Adapter with a personal Gmail account. It covers the necessary Gmail security setup, including enabling 2-Step Verification and generating an App Password for secure authentication. The guide also walks through the step-by-step configuration of the Mail Receiver Adapter in SAP Cloud Platform Integration (CPI) and demonstrates how to send emails successfully using the  Gmail account configured.

entry.title: Implementing Bulk Upsert using Snowflake Adapter in SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Overview 

In many integration projects, loading data into Snowflake is a common requirement. You may have used different approaches to insert or upsert data into Snowflake tables using Snowflake adapter depending on the system landscape and architecture : 

  • Insert operation in the adapter 
  • Executing SQL queries (Multi statements) 
  • Bulk Upsert Using External Stage (AWS S3, Azure Blob, or GCS) 

All these approaches work well. However, each may present itself with certain considerations.

For example: 

  • The Insert operation requires metadata configuration and may not be ideal for large datasets because constructing full INSERT statements can be overkilling while processing large datasets 
  • Executing queries means writing and managing SQL multi statements logic manually. 
  • External Stage requires hyperscaler storage (AWS, Azure, or GCS). 

Now let us assume a practical situation if the customer’s landscape does not include AWS, Azure, or GCS ?  

The latest version (1.2.0) of the adapter now also supports loading bulk data through  Snowflake Internal Stage. This is suggested for scenarios where user doesn’t have third-party storage services.

What’s New in the Snowflake Adapter?

The Snowflake Adapter now supports: 

  • File Staging operation (Put, List, Get) 
  • Bulk Upsert and Unload Operation with Access Type: Internal Stage 

With the above enhancements, you can: 

  • Upload, List and Fetch a file directly from Snowflake. 
  • Use that staged file to perform a bulk upsert into a target table. 
  • Unload the data from the Snowflake table to a stage location.

Let us first understand two important concepts before diving into the demo. 

What is File Staging in Snowflake? 

File staging in Snowflake is the process of uploading structured files (such as CSV or JSON) to a temporary storage location called a stage, from where Snowflake can efficiently load or merge the data into tables. 

Instead of inserting records one by one, data is processed in bulk using staged files, making it faster, scalable, and more suitable for large data loads. 

What is Snowflake Internal Stage? 

A Snowflake Internal Stage is a storage location managed entirely within Snowflake where files can be temporarily stored before being loaded into tables. 

It does not require AWS, Azure, or GCP storage and allows you to upload and process files directly inside Snowflake, making bulk data loading simpler and more streamlined. 

Integration Scenario : Bulk Upsert from Internal Stage  

Imagine a business scenario where a large volume of transactional records from an ERP system needs to be ingested into Snowflake for centralized storage, reporting, and analytics. 

  •  A straightforward approach would be to fetch the data from the ERP system and push it directly into Snowflake. However, when dealing with thousands or even lakhs of records, this method can take considerable time. 
  • Another option would be to write and execute custom SQL queries (such as Multi statements) to handle bulk processing. But maintaining large queries for high volumes of data is time-consuming and increases the risk of errors. 
  • Additionally, using an External Stage requires AWS, Azure, or GCP storage in the landscape. If the customer does not have a hyperscaler environment, that approach is simply not feasible. This is where the Internal Stage feature in the Snowflake Adapter becomes highly relevant.

In this blog, I will walk you through how to bulk insert records into Snowflake using the Snowflake Adapter in SAP Integration Suite. We will also briefly look at how the execution is handled internally within Snowflake for better understanding. 

Prerequisites

Before we begin, let us assume that: 

  • A target table with the required schema already exists in Snowflake. 
  • The connection between Snowflake and SAP Integration Suite is properly configured and working. 
  • Internal Stage is created in Snowflake. 

Let us now deep dive into the end-to-end implementation. 

Iflow Design in SAP CI 

For this example we have used a CSV file with 25k records in the Content Modifier which is uploaded to snowflake stage in the next step and then used for bulk upsert.

File Staging using Snowflake Adapter

The file is uploaded to the Snowflake Internal Stage using the PUT command. During configuration of the adapter, both the file name and the name of the Internal Stage must be specified. If required, a specific folder path within the stage can also be provided. Otherwise, the file will be uploaded to the default location of the Internal Stage. 
 

Bulk Upsert Using Snowflake Adapter

In this step, the data is upserted into the Snowflake table using the file that was previously staged in the Internal Stage. The configuration requires specifying the target table name and the Internal Stage name from which the data will be read. The file name should also be specified, as it determines which file’s data will be loaded into the table. If no file name is provided, the adapter will process all files available in the directory. Additionally, if the file is located within a specific subdirectory inside the stage, the corresponding path should be mentioned as well 
 
1. Also, note that Stages in Snowflake have a defined file format that determines which type of files can be uploaded and how those files are processed. Therefore, if you want to process file formats that are different from the one defined in the stage, you must provide the necessary parameters explicitly. 

For example, in the optional parameters field, you can specify: 

FILE_FORMAT = (TYPE = CSV) FORCE = TRUE ON_ERROR = CONTINUE

 2. Additionally, the File Name field can contain multiple files. For example: 

'BulkUpsert_Hundred_Rows1.csv','BulkUpsert_Hundred_Rows2.csv'

 3. Another important point to highlight is that if the file stored in the internal stage is compressed, the bulk upsert operation can automatically decompress the file and then load the data into the Snowflake table. 

For more details on optional parameters, please refer to the official Snowflake documentation: 
Optional parameters in Snowflake 

Now the iflow designing and configuration is complete, the iflow is ready to deploy. 

You can verify the same in the Snowflake application.
 
File is being staged in internal stage.  

 

File picked from stage and data upserted in snowflake table (EMP_DATA).  

Final Thoughts 

Using Internal Stage with the Snowflake Adapter makes bulk data loading into Snowflake simpler and more efficient, especially when external cloud storage is not part of the system landscape.

In our upcoming blog, we will also explore how to extract data from Snowflake tables via Snowflake Adapter. Stay tuned!

entry.title: Data Integration in ABAP Cloud: Data Consumption using External Entities
entry.content:
In the previous post, we discussed data exposure using SQL services. In this post, we shift the focus to the data consumption scenario.

Imagine an ABAP Cloud system that needs to access data located elsewhere, perhaps in an SAP HANA Cloud database, or even in another ABAP system. With SQL Services, an ABAP system can itself act as a database. To support such outbound data access in a clean and cloud-compliant way, ABAP Cloud introduces a new CDS modelling artifact: CDS External Entities.

 

What Are CDS External Entities?

CDS External Entities enable outbound SQL access from an ABAP Cloud system. This means you can:

  • Access data from a remote database
  • Load and process remote data in ABAP
  • Combine remote data with local data

In ABAP Cloud, classic secondary database connections are not available. CDS External Entities are their more powerful and cloud-ready replacement.

Conceptually, a CDS External Entity is comparable to an SAP HANA virtual table; it represents a local view of a remote table or view. The remote object and its fields may have different names, which can be mapped explicitly.

CDS External Entities can be consumed in two different ways:

- Dynamic Access

In the SAP BTP ABAP environment, a CDS External Entity can be used directly in ABAP SQL. You write a SELECT statement that accesses data from a remote database, loads it, and processes it in ABAP.

- Static Access

Starting with release 2508, CDS view entities and classic CDS views can be built on top of external entities. This allows you to integrate remote data seamlessly into your CDS view modelling and treat it like local data. This feature will be used in my next blog post of the series.

Logical External Schema

Alongside CDS External Entities, there is a second important artifact: the Logical External Schema which is based on SAP HANA Smart Data Access (SDA). The key features are as follows:

  • It represents a remote system at design time.
  • It is mapped to a concrete remote source at configuration time.
  • It contains connection-related information (host, port, user, etc.).

From a runtime perspective, the ABAP system sends the query to its own HANA database, which then federates the query to the remote system using SDA.

 

How External Entities Work at Runtime

External entities allow remote data access using a new ABAP SQL syntax:

SELECT FROM <external_entity>

  PROVIDED BY <logical_external_schema>

  INTO TABLE @DATA(result).

In this flow:

  1. The ABAP application sends the SQL query to the local HANA database.
  2. HANA performs query federation using Smart Data Access.
  3. Data is retrieved from the remote database.
  4. Results are returned to the ABAP application server.

The <logical_external_schema> is a design-time artifact that points to the remote system and is bound to an actual remote source during configuration.

 

Example Scenario

Let us build an example based on the travel scenario in which

  • SAP HANA Cloud acts as the remote database.
  • The ABAP system consumes data via Dynamic CDS External Entities.

It is crucial that

  • The external entity has the same name and field structure as the remote HANA table.
  • Field names and types must match exactly (HANA is case-sensitive).

Besides, to enable the access, an outbound service and a communication scenario are required.

Design Time Steps

1. Define a Dynamic CDS External Entity

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #CHECK
@EndUserText.label: 'external entity for travel'
define external entity ZI_EE_TRAVEL_000 external name TRAVEL
{
  key TravelID      : abap.numc(8)                external name "TravelID";
      AgencyID      : abap.numc(6)                external name AgencyID;
      CustomerID    : abap.numc(6)                external name CustomerID;
      @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'CurrencyCode'
      BookingFee    : abap.curr(16,2)             external name BookingFee;
      @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'CurrencyCode'
      TotalPrice    : abap.curr(16,2)             external name TotalPrice;
      CurrencyCode  : abap.cuky                   external name CurrencyCode;
      BeginDate     : abap.dats                   external name BeginDate;
      EndDate       : abap.dats                   external name EndDate;
      Description   : abap.sstring(1024)          external name Description;
      OverallStatus : abap.char(1)                external name OverallStatus;
      CreatedBy     : abp_creation_user           external name CreatedBy;
      CreatedAt     : abp_creation_tstmpl         external name CreatedAt;
      LastChangedBy : abp_locinst_lastchange_user external name LastChangedBy;
}
with federated data 
provided at runtime;

This entity models the structure of the remote table or view.

2. Define a Logical External Schema

This acts as a placeholder for the remote database and is referenced by the external entity.

3. Create Outbound Service and Communication Scenario

  • Create an SQL type outbound service for the logical external schema
  • Create a communication scenario and assign the outbound service to it. Then save and publish the scenario locally.

This enables administrators to configure access to the remote system

4. Consume the External Entity in ABAP SQL

Create a class and write ABAP SQL code

Select * from my external entity provided by my remote into table my local table

CLASS zcl_travel_consume_000 DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC .

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES if_oo_adt_classrun .
  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_travel_consume_000 IMPLEMENTATION.
METHOD if_oo_adt_classrun~main.
     data: travel type table of zi_ee_travel_000.

    select * from zi_ee_travel_000 provided by z_travel_remote_db_000 into table @Travel.

    out->write( travel ).
    endmethod.

ENDCLASS.

In summary, what happens at runtime is as follows

  • The ABAP system sends the query to its HANA database
  • HANA SDA forwards the query to the remote database
  • Data is fetched and returned to the ABAP application
  • The local internal table is filled with remote data

Now we need to establish the connection between our development system and the SAP HANA Cloud database.

 

Configuration Time Steps

1. Create Communication Arrangement

In the SAP Fiori Launchpad:

  • Create a communication arrangement for the communication scenario you published in design time.

2. Maintain a communication system for the remote database

  • Activate Remote SQL-Access
  • Choose HANA ODBC as the SDA adapter
  • Provide host, port, driver, and additional connection settings
    (These details are typically provided by the system administrator)

save the communication system.

3. Maintain Remote Schema Information

In the communication arrangement:

  • Specify the schema name of the remote HANA database
  • Save the configuration and check whether the connection is successful

4. Prepare the Remote HANA Database

In Visual Studio Code and using SAP HANA Database Explorer:

  • Create a table that matches the CDS External Entity exactly
  • Ensure field names, types, and sizes match as well

Remember that SAP HANA is case-sensitive.

What Happens Behind the Scenes

When you execute the ABAP SQL statement:

  • The ABAP SQL layer resolves the external entity and logical external schema
  • HANA automatically:
    • Creates a remote source
    • Creates the required virtual table
  • Query federation is handled transparently

In this sense, CDS External Entities act as a smart abstraction that automatically manages HANA remote sources and virtual tables via ABAP design-time objects.

Press F9, and the data is returned as expected.

In the next blog post I would like to build a RAP application in the cloud, based on data I read from an on-premises system. So, stay tuned!

entry.title: Event Mesh + AMQP: The Retry Trap Nobody Warns You About
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

I have seen a number of implementations of event-driven design involving SAP Event Mesh and the AMQP sender adapter on Cloud Integration. Most were built on the incorrect assumption that standard settings would be sufficient — without proper end-to-end testing to validate that assumption.

In this blog, I explain two patterns I have used in practice, along with their strengths and trade-offs. I have also added an important baseline section on why the native AMQP retry configuration alone is not enough.

Finally, we will look at whether Advanced Event Mesh requires the same workarounds — or whether it solves the problem out of the box.

Baseline: Why Native AMQP Retry Is Not Enough

The AMQP sender adapter does have a built-in Retry Details configuration (Max. Number of Retries + Delivery Status After Max. Retries). However, the native retry has a critical limitation: there is no configurable retry delay or backoff interval. The broker redelivers immediately and — if not set up correctly — indefinitely. This means a temporary downstream outage produces a burst of failures rather than graceful back-pressure.

Max Redelivery Count in Event Mesh queue configuration set as as EMPTY/0 or Max. Number of Retries in the AMQP sender adapter set as EMPTY results in indefinite, immediate redeliveries that flood both the EM queue and the CI tenant — a serious stability risk.

📝Remark: According to SAP Note 3269414, configuring a Dead Message Queue (DMQ) in Event Broker can help limit retries, but this is not affecting Event Mesh.

The practical hint: to effectively limit number of retries you need to set retry number > 0 in Event Mesh queue settings and AMQP adapter.

JMSXDeliveryCount header is auto-incremented only for internal AMQP retries, not overall number of retries (AMQP + Broker side). JMSRedelivered header switch from false to true. Therefore retry strategy cannot be designed inside Iflow similary to JMS (SAPJMSRetries header). A potential workaround is to use a custom header (please see the description in Option 2).

As you can see, we can limit the number of retries, but we can't set a solid retry interval. That's exactly why I wrote this blog post with two solutions.

Each pattern has its strengths and trade-offs. Let's dive in.

Option 1: Decouple AMQP from iFlow Logic via JMS

In my previous blog series on JMS, I compared whether to use JMS or an Event Broker directly:
JMS Mastery Series Part 3 — JMS in Production

Actually, both approaches can work in synergy for certain scenarios — retry handling being a prime example.

The core idea: use the AMQP adapter only to ingest the message from Event Mesh, immediately write it to a JMS queue, and let the JMS adapter handle all retry logic from that point forward. Event Mesh is decoupled from the processing logic as soon as the message lands in JMS. The iFlow finishes with COMPLETED status and the message is acknowledged and removed from the EM queue.

How it works

  • The AMQP sender adapter receives the message from Event Mesh.
  • The iFlow immediately writes the payload to a JMS queue, which acts as an internal buffer.
  • A second iFlow reads from JMS and executes the full processing logic.
  • JMS handles retries natively with a configurable interval and exponential backoff.

Pros

  • The Event Mesh connection is short-lived — released immediately after the JMS write, freeing EM resources.
  • Full JMS retry capability: configurable retry interval, exponential backoff, and maximum retry interval — none of which are available in the AMQP adapter.
  • Proven pattern — behaves identically to any JMS-backed HTTP or SOAP integration.

Cons

  • Consumes additional JMS resources — two queues are involved: the external EM queue and the internal JMS queue.
  • Increased operational overhead: more moving parts to monitor and maintain.

To reduce the number of JMS queues, you can assign one JMS queue per group of interfaces, or even one JMS queue for all interfaces from a single Event Mesh instance. However, this breaks the 1:1 mapping between EM queues and JMS queues, with important consequences.

💡Personal note: Even as a JMS advocate, I apply this pattern selectively. It is not the default. I add JMS on top of AMQP only when the iFlow logic is genuinely heavy — complex mappings, multi-step orchestration with high processing time or calls to multiple external systems (Recipient List pattern). Second use case is when you truly need robust retry control with exponential backoff. For operationally lightweight flows, Option 2 is cleaner.

Option 2: Retry Queue + TTL + DMQ (No JMS)

This pattern implements a retry-with-delay mechanism entirely within Event Mesh, using a dedicated Retry queue and TTL expiration to simulate retry backoff — without involving JMS at all.

How it works

  • The main iFlow catches errors in the exception subprocess.
  • If the error is transient and eligible for retry, the message is forwarded to a dedicated Retry queue in Event Mesh via an AMQP receiver adapter.
  • The Retry queue is configured with: DMQ = original processing queue, Max TTL = desired retry delay in seconds.
  • When the TTL expires, Event Mesh automatically moves the message to the DMQ — which is the original queue — and processing restarts.
  • The iFlow ends with an End Message event (COMPLETED status), so no automatic AMQP retry is triggered.

The TTL value effectively becomes your retry delay interval. You can implement increasing delays by chaining multiple retry queues with different TTL values, though this adds configuration complexity.

This creates a controlled retry loop: the message cycles between the Retry queue and the original queue until it either succeeds or the retry counter threshold is reached.

Tracking retry count

Because JMSXDeliveryCount resets to 1 and JMSRedelivered resets to false on every queue hop — this is a new delivery, not a broker redelivery — you cannot rely on these headers to count retry attempts.

Two approaches for maintaining a retry counter:

  • Custom header approach: add a RetryCount header to the message before forwarding it to the Retry queue, increment it on each pass, and stop retrying when it exceeds your threshold. This avoids any persistence layer and is the preferred approach for most scenarios.
  • Data Store approach: persist a counter keyed on a message correlation ID. This can introduce read/write lock contention at high throughput.

Pros

  • No JMS resources consumed.
  • Retry delay is cleanly controlled via TTL — no thread blocking.

Cons

  • The setup is non-obvious and requires careful EM queue configuration.
  • JMSXDeliveryCount and JMSRedelivered are unreliable for retry counting — custom counter logic is required.
  • A Data Store-based counter introduces contention risk at high throughput.
  • No native exponential backoff — fixed TTL only, unless you chain queues.

💡Personal note: This is my preferred option. Clean, JMS-free, and elegant once you understand the TTL mechanics.

Option 3 (Not Recommended): Groovy Sleep as Retry Delay

This is a workaround I have seen in the field: using a sleep() call inside a Groovy script to introduce a delay before retrying, relying on the AMQP adapter's built-in retry to re-execute the iFlow.

Pros

  • No additional infrastructure — no JMS, no Retry queue, no TTL configuration.
  • Simple to implement for a quick proof of concept.

Cons

  • The AMQP connection to Event Mesh remains open and locked for the entire sleep duration, preventing EM from reclaiming the resource.
  • The CI worker thread is blocked for the sleep duration. Under concurrent load, this exhausts the worker thread pool and degrades performance for all iFlows on the same tenant — not just this one.
  • sleep() in Groovy on Cloud Integration is a tenant-level anti-pattern. At scale, it can cause cascading timeouts across unrelated integrations. This is not merely an aesthetic concern — it is a production stability risk.
  • No retry limit control without additional explicit design.

💡Personal note: Even though it is simple to implement, never use this in production. It is a quick hack, but the connection lock makes it an anti-pattern for any scenario requiring scalability. I would not recommend it to anyone.

Pattern Comparison

Use this table to guide your pattern selection:

CriteriaOption 1: JMS BridgeOption 2: TTL + DMQOption 3: Groovy SleepAMQP Retry
JMS RequiredYesNoNoNo
Retry Delay Control✅Full — retry interval, exponential backoff, max retry interval✅Via TTL — retry interval only, no exponential backoff⚠️Via sleep command only❌Immediate redelivery only
Retry CounterSAPJMSRetries headerCustom header / Data StoreEM + AMQP settings or Custom header / Data StoreEM + AMQP settings or Custom header / Data Store
EM Connection DurationShort - decoupled from iFlow processingStandard - but prevents long-lived connections caused by retriesLong ⚠️Standard
High Throughput FitYes (with care)Limited if Data Store used as counterNoYes (with care)
Setup ComplexityMediumMediumLowLow
Recommended ForHeavy iFlow logic, strict retry needsMost event-driven cases — low-to-moderate iFlow logic, flat retry interval is sufficientNot recommended — development/POC use only at mostSimple flows only — needs redesign for production

Does Retrying in Advanced Event Mesh Work Out of the Box?

Short answer: yes — AEM retry logic works like a charm. This is not surprising given that Advanced Event Mesh is not an entirely new product, but rather a white-label of Solace PubSub+, a very mature messaging solution with years of production use behind it.

What Can We Expect from AEM and the AEM Sender Adapter in Cloud Integration?

The full spectrum of retry configuration is available directly at the adapter level:

  • Retry interval
  • Maximum retry interval
  • Exponential backoff multiplier
  • Maximum number of retries

All of the above are configured directly on the AEM adapter in Cloud Integration, and all work as expected. No surprises, no disappointments. It is the best of both worlds:

  • JMS-level retry interval configuration is available
  • Event Mesh-level retry limit configuration is available

AEM offers not only adapter-level retries (CI side) but also broker-level retries (Max Redelivery Count on the queue). After CI-side retries are exhausted, a failed status is sent back to AEM, which then redelivers the message according to its own Max Redelivery Count — so the total retry attempts is a product of both settings, not a simple sum. Factor this in when configuring both to avoid unexpectedly high total retry counts.
This works like described above if Acknowledgment mode is set to: Automatic On Exchange Completed and Settlement outcome: Failed.

If you are fortunate enough to be working with AEM on your project, you do not need to apply any of the workarounds described above or add JMS on top of your event-driven design purely for the sake of working retry logic.

Final Thoughts

These two patterns exist because the AMQP adapter's native retry has no configurable delay. Until SAP adds native retry backoff to the AMQP adapter — or Event Mesh provides broker-side delay queues as a first-class feature — these workarounds remain necessary for production-grade event-driven designs.

My default recommendations:

  • Start with Option 2 (TTL + DMQ) for most flows. It is clean, JMS-free, and operationally lightweight.
  • Escalate to Option 1 (JMS bridge) only when the iFlow processing logic is genuinely complex or you need exponential backoff.
  • Never use the Groovy Sleep approach in production.
  • If AEM is available, the standard adapter configuration gives you a complete, production-grade solution out of the box — no workarounds required.

Additional Notes for Production

Idempotency and duplicate handling: Complement your chosen retry pattern with idempotency handling to avoid duplicate processing in the target system. The best place to implement deduplication is the receiver application, not the middleware. Also note that the event broker does not guarantee exactly-once processing — you must design for duplicates and assume at-least-once delivery.

Define the final stop after retries: Always define what happens when retries are exhausted. Route the message to a Dead Message Queue (DMQ) or equivalent quarantine mechanism, and ensure there is an operational process for monitoring and reprocessing those messages.

Are you using any of these patterns? Have you found other approaches? Comments, questions, and observations are very welcome.

entry.title: Why Compound Employee Delta Integrations May Behave Differently After ONB 2.0 Migration
entry.content:
Introduction

The move from ONB 1.0 to ONB 2.0 results in a major architectural shift in the underlying data model of Onboarding. This change has downstream impacts even on existing integrations consuming Employee Central data. Many organisations assume that since Employee Central itself is not being reconfigured, any integrations to and from it are safe. However, as we will see, this assumption can sometimes lead to unexpected failures.
