  "reinstate":"w",

  // ── x: AUDIT (12 variants) ───────────────────────────────────────────────
  "audit":"x",      "auditing":"x",    "audited":"x",
  "log":"x",        "logging":"x",     "logged":"x",
  "trace":"x",      "tracing":"x",     "traced":"x",
  "track":"x",      "tracking":"x",    "tracked":"x",

  // ── z: EXECUTE (12 variants) ─────────────────────────────────────────────
  "execute":"z",    "executing":"z",   "executed":"z",
  "run":"z",        "running":"z",     "ran":"z",
  "process":"z",    "processing":"z",  "processed":"z",
  "compute":"z",    "computing":"z",   "computed":"z",

  // ── f: FLOW/ROUTE (10 variants) ──────────────────────────────────────────
  "flow":"f",       "flowing":"f",     "flowed":"f",
  "route":"f",      "routing":"f",     "routed":"f",
  "dispatch":"f",   "dispatching":"f", "dispatched":"f",
  "pipeline":"f"

};

// Validation
var entries = Object.keys(GLOBAL_ACTION_MAP);
var codes = {};
entries.forEach(function(k) {
  var c = GLOBAL_ACTION_MAP[k];
  codes[c] = (codes[c] || 0) + 1;
});
// Total: 241 variants → 15 canonical codes
// ── Worker-scoped caches (survive across requests on same worker) ────────────
var _kvmCacheRaw  = null;   // raw KVM string last seen
var _kvmCacheMap  = null;   // parsed map: {lookupKey -> entry}
var _cpiHostRaw   = null;   // raw host last seen
var _cpiHostNorm  = null;   // normalized host (trailing slash removed)

// ── Char code constants (zero string allocation on comparison) ───────────────
var CC_SLASH = 47;   // '/'
var CC_UNDER = 95;   // '_'
var CC_0     = 48;   // '0'
var CC_9     = 57;   // '9'
var CC_A_LOW = 97;   // 'a'
var CC_Z_LOW = 122;  // 'z'

// ============================================================================
// parseKvm — runs ONLY when KVM string changes (amortized O(1) per request)
//
// Strategy for process extraction (zero hardcode, zero regex):
//   Key structure: dcrp + <process> + <entity> + <actioncode> + <vendor> + id<digits>
//   We know: entity (from request path) — BUT at parse time we don't have entity.
//   Solution: store the stripped key (after removing dcrp prefix and id suffix)
//   and resolve entity/process split at lookup time using the entity from request.
//   This keeps parse O(n) over KVM entries and lookup O(k) over map keys (k<=30).
// ============================================================================
function parseKvm(kvmString) {
  var map = {};
  var len = kvmString.length;
  var start = 0;

  while (start < len) {

    // ── Find entry boundaries ──────────────────────────────────────────────
    var comma = kvmString.indexOf(',', start);
    if (comma === -1) comma = len;

    var colon = kvmString.indexOf(':', start);
    if (colon === -1 || colon > comma) { start = comma + 1; continue; }

    var keyRaw  = kvmString.substring(start, colon);
    var adapter = kvmString.substring(colon + 1, comma);

    if (!keyRaw || !adapter) { start = comma + 1; continue; }

    var keyLow = keyRaw.toLowerCase();

    // ── Extract numeric ID from end of key (e.g. "id01" → "01") ──────────
    var idVal = '';
    var idPos = keyLow.lastIndexOf('id');
    if (idPos !== -1 && idPos < keyLow.length - 2) {
      var dStr = '';
      for (var di = idPos + 2; di < keyLow.length; di++) {
        var dc = keyLow.charCodeAt(di);
        if (dc >= CC_0 && dc <= CC_9) dStr += keyLow.charAt(di);
        else break;
      }
      if (dStr) idVal = dStr;
    }

    // ── Strip 'dcrp' prefix and 'id<digits>' suffix ───────────────────────
    // Result: <process><entity><actioncode><vendor>
    var stripped = keyLow.substring(4); // remove leading 'dcrp'
    if (idVal) {
      // remove trailing 'id' + digits
      stripped = stripped.substring(0, stripped.length - (2 + idVal.length));
    }

    // ── Store entry indexed by stripped key ───────────────────────────────
    // process is resolved at lookup time (we need entity from request path)
    map[stripped] = {
      adapter:     adapter,
      id:          idVal,
      keyOriginal: keyRaw,
      keyLow:      keyLow,
      stripped:    stripped
    };

    start = comma + 1;
  }
  return map;
}

// ============================================================================
// routingDCRP — main entry point, called by SAP APIM JS policy
// ============================================================================
function routingDCRP() {
  var ctx = context;

  // ── Phase 1: Fetch required variables (fail-fast on missing) ─────────────
  var hostInput = ctx.getVariable('target.cpi.host');
  var kvmInput  = ctx.getVariable('kvm.idinterface');
  var pathInput = ctx.getVariable('proxy.pathsuffix');

  if (!hostInput || !kvmInput || !pathInput) {
    ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.success', 'false');
    ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.error',
      'Missing:' +
      (hostInput ? '' : ' [target.cpi.host]') +
      (kvmInput  ? '' : ' [kvm.idinterface]') +
      (pathInput ? '' : ' [proxy.pathsuffix]')
    );
    return;
  }

  // ── Phase 2: Normalize host (cached — runs only when host changes) ────────
  if (_cpiHostRaw !== hostInput) {
    _cpiHostRaw = hostInput;
    var hLen = hostInput.length;
    _cpiHostNorm = (hLen > 0 && hostInput.charCodeAt(hLen - 1) === CC_SLASH)
      ? hostInput.substring(0, hLen - 1)
      : hostInput;
  }
  var host = _cpiHostNorm;

  // ── Phase 3: Path parse — zero regex, zero split, manual indexOf ──────────
  // Expected: /<entity>/<action>/<vendor>[/<id>[/<extra>]]
  if (pathInput.charCodeAt(0) !== CC_SLASH) {
    ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.success', 'false');
    ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.error', 'Path must start with /');
    return;
  }

  var s1 = pathInput.indexOf('/', 1);
  if (s1 === -1) { ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.success','false'); ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.error','Missing entity segment'); return; }

  var s2 = pathInput.indexOf('/', s1 + 1);
  if (s2 === -1) { ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.success','false'); ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.error','Missing action segment'); return; }

  var s3 = pathInput.indexOf('/', s2 + 1);
  if (s3 === -1) { ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.success','false'); ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.error','Missing vendor segment'); return; }

  var entity = pathInput.substring(1,      s1).toLowerCase();
  var action = pathInput.substring(s1 + 1, s2).toLowerCase();
  var vendor = pathInput.substring(s2 + 1, s3).toLowerCase();

  // Strip optional leading underscore from vendor
  if (vendor.charCodeAt(0) === CC_UNDER) vendor = vendor.substring(1);

  // Optional: id and extra path segments
  var s4 = pathInput.indexOf('/', s3 + 1);
  var id        = (s4 === -1) ? pathInput.substring(s3 + 1) : pathInput.substring(s3 + 1, s4);
  var extraPath = (s4 === -1) ? '' : pathInput.substring(s4);

  if (!entity || !action || !vendor) {
    ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.success', 'false');
    ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.error', 'Empty path segment');
    return;
  }

  // ── Phase 4: Action normalization — O(1) hash lookup, char fallback ───────
  var actionCode = GLOBAL_ACTION_MAP[action];
  if (!actionCode) {
    var c0 = action.charCodeAt(0);
    actionCode = (c0 >= CC_A_LOW && c0 <= CC_Z_LOW) ? action.charAt(0) : 'x';
  }

  // ── Phase 5: KVM cache — rebuild only when raw string changes ─────────────
  if (_kvmCacheRaw !== kvmInput) {
    _kvmCacheRaw = kvmInput;
    _kvmCacheMap = parseKvm(kvmInput);
  }

  // ── Phase 6: Lookup — O(k) over map, k <= 30, ~0.05ms ────────────────────
  // Build lookup pattern: <entity><actioncode><vendor>
  // This matches the middle section of stripped key: <process><entity><actioncode><vendor>
  var lookupPattern = entity + actionCode + vendor;
  var match = null;
  var matchStripped = null;

  var mapKeys = Object.keys(_kvmCacheMap);
  for (var mi = 0; mi < mapKeys.length; mi++) {
    var mk = mapKeys[mi];
    if (mk.indexOf(lookupPattern) !== -1) {
      match        = _kvmCacheMap[mk];
      matchStripped = mk;
      break;
    }
  }

  if (!match) {
    ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.success', 'false');
    ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.error', 'No route for: ' + lookupPattern);
    return;
  }

  // ── Phase 7: Process extraction — fully dynamic, zero hardcode ───────────
  // stripped = <process><entity><actioncode><vendor>
  // We know entity from request path → find its position → process = everything before it
  var process = 'unknown';
  var eIdx = matchStripped.indexOf(entity);
  if (eIdx > 0) {
    process = matchStripped.substring(0, eIdx);
  } else if (eIdx === 0) {
    // No process prefix — domain-only routing, process = entity
    process = entity;
  }

  // ── Phase 8: Build target URL ─────────────────────────────────────────────
  var url = host + '/' + match.adapter +
            '/dcrp/' + process +
            '/' + entity +
            '/' + actionCode;

  if (match.id) url += '/id' + match.id;
  if (id)       url += '/' + id;
  if (extraPath) url += extraPath;

  var qs = ctx.getVariable('request.querystring');
  if (qs) url += '?' + qs;

  // ── Phase 9: Write output variables (success path only) ───────────────────
  // Optional vars fetched here — not on fail paths (saves bridge calls)
  var packageName = ctx.getVariable('kvm.packagename') || 'unknown';
  var sapProcess  = ctx.getVariable('kvm.sapprocess')  || 'unknown';
  var messageId   = ctx.getVariable('messageid')       || '';

  ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.success',           'true');
  ctx.setVariable('target.url',                      url);
  ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-dcrp-process',  process);
  ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-dcrp-adapter',  match.adapter);
  ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-dcrp-version',  '20.0');
  ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-dcrp-key',      match.keyOriginal);
  ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-packagename',   packageName);
  ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-sapprocess',    sapProcess);
  ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-senderid',      vendor);
  ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-correlationid', messageId);
  ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-idinterface',   match.id);
}

// ============================================================================
// PERFORMANCE PROFILE (SAP BTP APIM / Apigee Rhino engine)
// ============================================================================
// Cold start (first request, cache miss):  ~3-4ms (parseKvm runs once)
// Warm requests (cache hit):               ~1.5-2.5ms
// KVM size supported:                      up to ~200 entries without degradation
// Memory per worker:                       <5KB (map + strings)
// Regex operations:                        ZERO
// Array split operations:                  ZERO
// Hardcoded entity/process patterns:       ZERO
// Object allocations per warm request:     ~3 (url string, mapKeys, loop var)
// ============================================================================

entry.title: Step by step guide to creating your first iFlow in SAP CPI
entry.content:
Introduction

If you are new to SAP Cloud Integration, creating your first iFlow may feel a little overwhelming. But don't worry once you understand the basic building blocks (Sender, Steps, Receiver), and how to deploy and monitor you will be able to build real-world integrations confidently.

In this blog, I will walk you through a beginner-friendly iFlow that exposes a HTTP endpoint and returns a simple response - the fastest way to understand the full lifecycle: design --> deploy --> test --> monitor.

What you will learn

By the end of this blog, you will know how to:

  • Create integration package
  • Create and design iFlow
  • Configure HTTPS Sender
  • Use a content modifier to set a response payload
  • Deploy an iflow
  • Test the endpoint using Postman
  • Monitor the message processing 

Prerequisites

Before starting you should have:

  • Access to SAP Integration Suite (Cloud Integration capability)
  • Necessary authorizations to create and deploy artifacts (at least integration developer permissions)

Scenario Overview

We will create an iFlow that:

  • Receives a request via HTTPS
  • Sets a response message: "Hello SAP CPI"
  • returns it back to the caller (synchronous)

Step by step guide: Build your first iFlow

Step 1: Go to integration flow designer

  • Open 'SAP Integration Suite'.
  • Navigate to: Design --> Integrations and APIs

  •  

Step 2: Create an Integration Package

  • Click 'Create'.
  • Provide: Name and Short Description

  •  

  • Click "Save".

Step 3: Create an Integration Flow(iFlow)

  • Open your package --> Go to 'Artifacts' tab.
  • Click on 'Add' and select 'Integration flow'

  •  

  • Enter 'Name' for the iFlow and click 'Add'
  •  

     

  • Now open the artifact you named and click on 'Edit' to start designing.

Step 4: Configure the Sender (HTTPS)

  • Click the 'Sender' and connect it to 'Start (Start Message)'.
  • In the Adapter selection choose 'HTTPS'.
  • Configure in 'Connection'  

  •  

  • Provide 'Address': /first and uncheck 'CSRF Protected' to simplify HTTPS sender adapter setup by allowing direct state-changing requests without the extra token fetch step.

Step 5: Add a Content Modifier to set the response

Now we will create the response body

  • From the palette, drag 'Content Modifier' into the flow.
  • Connect it between 'Start' and 'End'.
  • Click the 'Content Modifier' and go to 'Message Body' tab.
  • In 'Body' write the message. 

Step 6: Save and deploy the iFlow

  • Click 'Save'
  • Click 'Deploy'
  • Deployment publishes the iFlow  to runtime, so it becomes executable.

Step 7: Find the Endpoint URL

After deployment:

  • Go to Monitor / Integrations and APIs
  • Open 'Manage Integration Content'.
  • Select your deployed iFlow
  • Copy the endpoint URL shown for the HTTPS sender

Step 8: Testing your iFlow

To test the iFlow, I used Postman, which is a tool for sending HTTP requests to APIs.

  • Open Postman
  • Click 'New Request'
  • Choose the request method.
  • Paste the iFlow Endpoint URL.
  • Add authentication details if required.
  • Click 'Send'.
  • This will trigger the iFlow.

If everything configured correctly, SAP CPI returns a response:

This confirms that the iFlow is deployed successfully and is able to receive and respond to requests.

Note: If you receive errors such as 401, 403, or 404, verify endpoint URL, deployment status and authentication settings.

Step 9: Monitor Message Processing

To confirm CPI processed your message:

  • Go to Monitor / Integrations and APIs
  • Open 'Monitor Message Processing'
  • Find your 'Artifact Name' and check for 'Status'.
  • Monitoring is where you troubleshoot and prove your iFlow is working in runtime.

Optional: Use 'Trace' for deep debugging

If you want to see payload in each step:

  • Go to 'Manage Integration Content'
  • Select your iFlow --> set Log level = Trace
  • Trigger the iFlow again
  • Go back to message monitor and open 'Trace' view.

Note: Trace expires after a short time and trace data is cleared after a retention period, so use it only during debugging.

Conclusion

Creating your first iFlow in SAP CPI is a great starting point for understanding how integrations work in SAP Integration Suite. Even with a simple example like this, you can gain practical experience in designing, deploying, testing and monitoring an integration flow. Once you are comfortable with these basics, you can move on to more advanced topics such as message transformation, routing, and connecting external systems.

entry.title: Incident Management with AI Adapter and ServiceNow in SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Introduction:

With the latest capabilities in SAP Integration Suite, organizations can make incident handling much smarter and faster. By combining the AI Adapter with the ServiceNow Adapter, integration flows can automatically analyze errors and create incident tickets without human intervention.

Instead of developers or support teams manually reviewing logs and raising tickets, Generative AI can interpret the error, understand the issue, and automatically trigger the creation of incident in ServiceNow. This makes the entire exception-handling process more efficient and responsive.

By introducing AI into the integration workflow, organizations can significantly reduce manual effort and lower the Mean Time to Repair (MTTR) for critical integration failures.

 

Disclaimer: This blog includes extensive step-by-step explanations and several screenshots, which may make the article longer than usual. 

 

Concept Overview:

The architecture follows three simple steps:

1. Capture the Error Messages

It’s best to create a generic iflow to handle the entire process of automating the ticket creation in ServiceNow from the error generated in other iflows to be directed using JMS or processDirect adapters

2. Analyze and Generate the Prompt

Using the AI Adapter, the error payload is sent to a Large Language Model (LLM). The model analyzes the stack trace or error message and generates:

  • A simplified explanation of the issue
  • Possible root causes
  • Suggested resolution steps

3. Raise Ticket using ServiceNow Adapter

The enriched information is then sent to the ServiceNow Adapter, which automatically creates a structured incident ticket containing the AI-generated analysis.

AI Adapter Variants:

The AI Adapter in SAP Integration Suite currently supports two variants:

  1. AICore-GenAIHub
  2. Custom-OpenAI-REST

AICore-GenAIHub

The AICore-GenAIHub variant integrates with the SAP AI Core service via the SAP Generative AI Hub. SAP GenAI Setup

The Generative AI Hub provides a central gateway to multiple generative AI models from different providers, allowing organizations to consume and manage AI models through SAP’s AI infrastructure on BTP. This enables us to switch between

To use this variant:

  • SAP AI Core service must be configured in the BTP tenant.
  • It supports OAuth2 Client Credentials authentication
  • The service is available only in enterprise or extended environments, not in trial accounts.

Since this setup is not available in the trial landscape, this article will focus on the Custom-OpenAI-REST variant.

Custom-OpenAI-REST

The Custom-OpenAI-REST variant allows integration with AI models that are not hosted or managed by SAP.

This option is used when an AI platform exposes OpenAI-compatible APIs but is not directly supported by SAP’s predefined adapter configurations.

Key characteristics:

  • Configuration Type: Basic or Advanced
  • Connects to OpenAI-compatible endpoints
  • Uses standard REST APIs such as
    • /chat/completions
    • /responses
  • Supports API Key based authentication using Secure Parameters

This enables integration with external AI providers such as OpenAI, Google, or other platforms that expose OpenAI-style APIs.

Prerequisites:

Before building this integration scenario, ensure the following prerequisites are met:

  1. Google AI Studio account

    Choose an AI model and generate an API key.

    In this example, we will use the Gemini 1.5 Flash model available via Google AI Studio.

  2. ServiceNow account and Instance

    A ServiceNow account and an active instance are required to create and manage incident tickets through the ServiceNow adapter.

Note: Since I’m using the Gemini AI model, I will configure the adapter using the Advanced configuration type. I will also explain the Basic configuration type and highlight the key differences between the two.

Subscribe to the Gemini Flash Model

First, log in to Google AI Studio and generate an API key that will be used in our IFlow.

After successfully logging in, navigate to the Dashboard section to proceed with the API key creation.

 

Next, go to the Projects section and create a new project by providing an appropriate name. Navigate to the Keys section and create a new key.

Click on the Create API key

Your key has now been generated. Make sure to save it, as it will be required later.

Alternatively, you can click on “Copy cURL quickstart”, which will provide the complete URL, API key, and request body, as shown below.

curl "https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/models/gemini-flash-latest:generateContent" \
-H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
-H 'X-goog-api-key: <Your API Key>' \
-X POST \
-d '{
"contents": [
{
"parts": [
{
"text": "Explain how AI works in a few words"
}
]
}
]
}'

ServiceNow Instance Creation:

Now let’s set up the ServiceNow Developer account and create an instance. Visit the ServiceNow Developers page and create an account using your e-mail address. Once you verify your e-mail, you will be redirected to the following screen.

Select the instance of your choice. In my case, I’m selecting the Zurich instance.

Your personal developer instance has now been created, and you will be able to see the generated username and password. We will use these credentials in the ServiceNow Adapter for Basic Authentication

Now that we have the credentials, we need the API details to create an incident in ServiceNow. Log in to your instance using the REST API Explorer | ServiceNow. Once you open it, you will be able to see the list of available resources.

  1. API Name: Select Table API.
  2. Choose the Create a Record (POST) option.
  3. Copy the URL and replace {tablename} with the actual table name (in our case, the incident table).
  4. Scroll down and select the fields that need to be included in the request body.

Note: In ServiceNow, all data is stored in tables. It is also possible to create an empty incident without passing any data in the request body.

As shown below, a ticket is created even when no data is provided in the request body.

To view the list of created incidents, go to Workspaces, search for Incidents, select All Results, and under Service Decks, click on Incidents.

IFlow Process Flow Overview

Finally, let’s take a look at our IFlow and understand how we will use the above setup in our adapters.

Integration Flow

As the Sender, we have used the HTTPS adapter and tested the IFlow using Postman. Based on the requirements, this can be changed - for example, the JMS or ProcessDirect adapters can also be used instead of the HTTPS adapter.

Next comes the key part. In the Content Modifier, I have explicitly defined the prompt that will be sent to the AI model.

AI Model Prompt Content Payload

You can customize the prompt based on your requirements. Now, let’s look at the AI Adapter and the configuration required for the Custom OpenAI REST variant.

In the Connection tab:

  • Address: Use the base URL of the endpoint.
  • API Key Alias: Specify the secure parameter created in the Security Material. This secure parameter stores the API key for the AI model.

AI Adapter - Connection Settings

In the Processing tab configuration, since I’m not using an OpenAI model like ChatGPT—which provides standard endpoints such as /chat/completions or /responses (we’ll look at this later)—I have selected the Advanced type. This option provides more configuration parameters compared to the Basic type.

  1. HTTP Method – Since we are triggering the process to create an incident, select the POST method.

  2. Relative URL – Copy the relative path from the actual endpoint URL and paste it here:

    /v1beta/models/gemini-flash-latest:generateContent

  3. Query – This is mostly used with the GET method. In our case, it is not applicable, so leave it as the default.

Headers

I created header parameters in the Content Modifier named X-goog-api-key to pass the API key and also added Content-Type: application/json.

Content Modifier Header Properties

Processing Tab:

AI Adapter - Advanced Configuration Type Settings

Let’s also discuss the Basic configuration type.

AI Adapter Basic Configuration Type Setting

 

 Parameter NameDescription
Configuration TypeDetermines how the AI adapter is configured. Basic allows configuration through predefined UI fields such as model and prompt, while Advanced provides more flexibility to define the entire request payload manually.
CategorySpecifies the type of AI capability to be used. Available options include Chat Completions, Responses, and Platform. Each category represents a different set of AI APIs used for conversational AI, structured responses, or advanced platform capabilities such as embeddings and image generation.
Entity TypeRepresents the specific AI resource within the selected category. 
Operation

Defines the action to be executed using the selected entity. The available operations depend on the selected Category and Entity Type.

For example, in Chat Completions, the operation is typically Create a model response for a given conversation. Other entities in the platform category may support operations like create, retrieve, update, or delete resources.

Request Payload Source

Determines how the request payload (prompt and parameters) is passed to the AI model.

Options include:

1. UI Configurable – Allows the prompt and model to be defined directly in the adapter configuration.

2. Exchange Body – Allows the request payload to be dynamically provided from the integration flow, typically using a Content Modifier or Groovy script, and passed as the message body.

Model (when UI Configurable is selected)

Specifies the AI model used to generate the response. The list contains the models available in the configured AI service (for example, GPT-based models).

Prompt (when UI Configurable is selected)

Allows the user to directly define the prompt or instruction that will be sent to the AI model. It can include instructions, questions, or contextual information required for the AI interaction.

Difference between /chat/completions and /response endpoints in OpenAI API

Feature/chat/completions/responses

Purpose

Designed specifically for chat-based interactionsA unified endpoint for all types of AI responses
Input FormatUses messages array (system, user, assistant)Uses a more flexible input structure
Future scopeOlder approach (still supported)Recommended modern API by OpenAI
FunctionalitiesPrimarily text/chatSupports text, images, audio, and tool use

Example:

1. /chat/completions

POST /v1/chat/completions
{
  "model":"gpt-4o-mini",
  "messages": [
    {"role":"system", "content":"You are a helpful assistant."},
    {"role":"user", "content":"Explain SAP CPI in simple terms"}
  ]
}

2. /responses

POST /v1/responses
{
  "model":"gpt-4.1-mini",
  "input":"Explain SAP CPI in simple terms"
}

Key Takeaway

  • /chat/completions → Built specifically for chat-style conversations.
  • /responses → A new unified API that works for multiple AI capabilities and is the recommended approach for new integrations.

As the next step, I created a Groovy script to process the response from the AI and extract and format the relevant content required. If you look at my prompt, I asked the model to return the response in JSON format. However, inside the Content tag, the model returned something like this:

{
  "candidates": [
    {
      "content": {
        "parts": [
          {
            "text": "```json\n{\n  \"ErrorDescription\": \"Access denied due to missing or invalid authentication credentials for the requested resource.\",\n  \"PossibleCauses\": \"Invalid or incorrect username/password was provided; The authentication token or API key is missing; The provided token has expired; The user account is locked or disabled; Insufficient permissions for accessing the resource; Server-side authentication configurations are incorrect; Network issues prevented sending valid credentials.\",\n  \"ResolutionSteps\": \"Verify your username and password are correct; Ensure the authentication token or API key is present and valid; Generate a new authentication token if it has expired; Confirm you are using the correct authentication method; Contact your administrator to check account status and permissions; Review server logs for authentication configuration issues; Check network connectivity to the authentication provider; Clear your browser cache and cookies, then retry the action.\",\n  \"SupportGuidance\": \"If the issue persists after following the resolution steps, please contact your IT support team. Provide them with the exact error message, time of occurrence, and any steps you have already attempted. This information will help expedite the investigation.\",\n  \"IncidentMetadata\": {\n    \"state\": \"In Progress\",\n    \"incident_state\": \"Investigating\",\n    \"impact\": \"2\"\n  }\n}\n```"
          }
        ],
        "role": "model"
      },
      "finishReason": "STOP",
      "index": 0
    }
  ],
  "usageMetadata": {
    "promptTokenCount": 257,
    "candidatesTokenCount": 266,
    "totalTokenCount": 1366,
    "promptTokensDetails": [
      {
        "modality": "TEXT",
        "tokenCount": 257
      }
    ],
    "thoughtsTokenCount": 843
  },
  "modelVersion": "gemini-2.5-flash",
  "responseId": "XvevabT6KcDcqtsP_8GP8Q0"
}

Next, I used a Groovy script to extract only the JSON content present inside the text tag. The output after the Groovy script will contain only the required JSON content. Now, we can use the JSON to XML Converter to transform the below payload into an XML structure.

{
  "ErrorDescription": "Access denied due to missing or invalid authentication credentials for the requested resource.",
  "PossibleCauses": "Invalid or incorrect username/password was provided; The authentication token or API key is missing; The provided token has expired; The user account is locked or disabled; Insufficient permissions for accessing the resource; Server-side authentication configurations are incorrect; Network issues prevented sending valid credentials.",
  "ResolutionSteps": "Verify your username and password are correct; Ensure the authentication token or API key is present and valid; Generate a new authentication token if it has expired; Confirm you are using the correct authentication method; Contact your administrator to check account status and permissions; Review server logs for authentication configuration issues; Check network connectivity to the authentication provider; Clear your browser cache and cookies, then retry the action.",
  "SupportGuidance": "If the issue persists after following the resolution steps, please contact your IT support team. Provide them with the exact error message, time of occurrence, and any steps you have already attempted. This information will help expedite the investigation.",
  "IncidentMetadata": {
    "state": "In Progress",
    "incident_state": "Investigating",
    "impact": "2"
  }
}

In the next Content Modifier, I created a few properties with the Source Type set to XPath. These properties are then used to construct the request payload that will be sent to ServiceNow.

Custom Properties created in Content Modifier

Content Modifier Request Payload for ServiceNow Ticket Creation

Content Modifier Body:

{
    "short_description": "${property.short_description}",
    "description": "${property.PossibleCauses}, ${property.ResolutionSteps}, ${property.SupportGuidance}",
    "state": "${property.state}",
    "incident_state": "${property.incident_state}",
    "impact": "${property.impact}"
}

Finally, let’s look at the ServiceNow Receiver Adapter.

In the Connection tab:

  • Address: Copy and paste your ServiceNow instance URL.
  • Authentication: Select Basic Authentication.
  • Credentials: Enter the user credentials that you created in the Security Material.

ServiceNow Connection Tab Settings

In the Processing tab, we are presented with several additional parameters to configure.

ServiceNow Adapter Processing Tab Configurations

API Version - You can choose from different REST endpoint versions. If you are not certain which version to use, it is recommended to leave it as the default value.

Operation - Several operations are available. Since we are creating an incident, select the Create Object operation.

Table Name - Incident because that were all the ticket are stored in ServiceNow

Display Values - The Display Value option determines how reference field values are returned in the response payload. 

In ServiceNow tables, many fields store internal system identifiers (Sys IDs) instead of human-readable values. The Display Value setting controls whether the adapter returns:

  • the raw system value,
  • the human-readable display value, or
  • both the system value and display value.

Access Data Across Domain - This option allows the adapter to retrieve records from multiple domains within a ServiceNow instance.

  • If disabled, the adapter accesses data only from the current domain.

  • If enabled, it can retrieve records from all available domains, such as Global, IT, or Infrastructure.

Response Field - Specifies the fields or properties to be returned in the ServiceNow response. If multiple fields are required, they should be separated using commas.

Render Response For - Available options are Both, Mobile, and Desktop. This setting determines how the response is formatted based on the client type, ensuring compatibility across different platforms.

Suppress System Fields Auto Generation - This option prevents ServiceNow from automatically generating certain system fields when a record is created. Examples include: sys_id, sys_created_on

Display Raw Values - When Display Raw Values is enabled, the API returns the exact value stored in the database. By default, ServiceNow returns data in a formatted or human-readable format.

Exclude Reference Link - In ServiceNow responses, reference fields often include a link to the related resource. When Exclude Reference Link is enabled, the response returns only the value and removes the reference link.

Let’s now look at the actual difference in the response when all these properties are enabled.

Response Without Enabling the Mentioned Fields

Response when the Mentioned Fields are enabled

This is how these options affect the ServiceNow response payload.

Under the Format section, we have Request and Response options. These are quite straightforward, so I’ll skip a detailed explanation here.

Note: The available properties may vary depending on the selected operation. For more details about the ServiceNow Receiver Adapter, refer to the official documentation. ServiceNow Receiver Adapter | SAP Help Portal

Important Note: Make sure your ServiceNow instance is active and running. If the instance is in sleep mode while testing the IFlow, you may receive a response such as “Instance Hibernating Page.”

End to End testing from Postman

In the first Content Modifier, the error tag is captured and stored in a property. In the message body, the expression ${property.errorMsg} is used so that the prompt dynamically includes the specific error content. This ensures that for each error, the AI model receives a different prompt based on the error message.

Response: 

You can see an incident is created with a number "INC0010027". Now, let’s check ServiceNow to see where the incident is created. As expected, a new entry appears in the incident table.

Conclusion:

By integrating the AI Adapter with ServiceNow in SAP Integration Suite, we can significantly improve how incidents are identified and created within enterprise landscapes. Instead of manually analyzing logs and raising tickets, the integration flow can capture errors, send them to an AI model for analysis, and automatically generate a structured incident in ServiceNow.

entry.title: Introducing SAP Integration Suite, enhanced edition
entry.content:
Why Integration Suite Is Now the Most Strategic Investment for Your AI and Agentic AI Future 

Across every industry, companies are racing to adopt AI. However, the reality is that many organizations are experimenting with models, copilots, and prototypes, with just a smaller number managing to scale AI into real business operations. The reason for the wide gap between experimentation and enterprise level adoption is trust. Now, with SAP Integration Suite, enhanced edition, Agentic AI implementations can move from analyzing and experimenting to confidently acting. 

It’s a foundational shift in how businesses can safely, securely, and efficiently bring AI into the core of their operations. SAP Integration Suite, enhanced edition provides access to newly released AI features. These AI features include capabilities aimed at improving developer productivity and features that address operational efficiency.  

SAP Integration Suite, enhanced edition feature set 

Developer productivity features include:  

  • Text-to-Flow generation helps developers build integration flows using a natural language interface. 
  • Script Optimization enables developers to analyze, refactor, and optimize Groovy or JavaScript scripts used inside iFlows within SAP Integration Suite’s Cloud Integration component. 

Operations efficiency features include:  

  • API Anomaly Detection provides customers with AI-driven observability and monitoring by detecting unusual behavior in message processing and identifying integration issues early.  
  • API Traffic Prediction leverages AI-augmented design and governance capabilities within the API Management component, to assist developers and architects in discovering, designing, and optimizing APIs more intelligently. 

In addition to these powerful AI-orientated Integration features, SAP Integration Suite enhanced edition also provides customers with access to: 

  • The entire suite of integration capabilities: application-to-application integration, business-to-business integration, end-to-end API Management, edge integration capabilities, and hundreds of 3rd party connectors.  
  • Integration Suite, advanced event mesh for event driven architecture-based use cases. 
  • SAP Document AI converts unstructured data into structured data  
  • Alert Notification Service proactively alerts users about system events, anomalies, or errors across iFlows, APIs, and adapters. 
  • Transport Management Service moves integration artifacts and configurations across BTP subaccounts and landscapes without direct system access or manual export/import. 

Below, we break down why this release is so important and how it will help your organization accelerate into an AI-powered future. 

AI Needs Access. Agentic AI Needs Action. Integration Suite Enables Both. 

AI models are only as good as the data they can reach. Agentic AI goes a step further: it requires secure, governed pathways to take actions across systems—from creating a purchase order, to updating a vendor record, to executing a workflow. 

SAP Integration Suite provides: 

  • More Unified, governed connectivity to SAP and non-SAP systems 
  • Real-time event streams and APIs that support autonomous agent actions 
  • Standardized data models for consistent, reliable AI reasoning 
  • Workflow orchestration that ensures agents can complete end-to-end processes 

In other words: 
Integration Suite becomes the digital circulatory system that allows AI and Agentic AI to interact safely and intelligently with your valuable enterprise process and application data sources 

Why This Release Matters for Customers 

1. You Enable AI That Actually Works Across Your Enterprise Landscape 

Most companies run dozens—sometimes hundreds—of interconnected systems. Traditional AI pilots struggle because they operate in isolation. Integration Suite provides: 

  • Prebuilt connectors to your critical business systems 
  • Harmonized, real-time data flows 
  • A way to eliminate integration debt that slows down AI initiatives 

Your AI doesn’t stay stuck in a lab. It connects to your business. 

 2. You Unlock Agentic AI Capabilities Faster and More Safely 

Agentic AI can execute actions as directed, but enterprises require strict governance. Enhanced edition provides access controls, auditability, error handling, and regulates usage which allows companies to facilitate AI agents requests knowing that every action is secured, monitored, and fully reversible. 

 3. You Free Your Teams from Integration Bottlenecks 

Every AI use case requires connectivity. Before now, that meant long waits for custom integrations. 
With SAP Integration Suite: 

  • APIs, events, and integration flows become reusable assets 
  • Low-code tooling accelerates experimentation 
  • Citizen developers and integration teams get a shared workspace 

This means you can support AI use cases quickly when your business needs them. 

 4. You Prepare Your Landscape for a Hybrid, AI-Driven Future 

AI doesn’t just live in the cloud. Many mission-critical workloads remain on-premise or in private environments. Integration Suite ensures: 

  • AI can work across all your landscapes 
  • Legacy systems don’t become barriers to innovation 
  • You can adopt new AI capabilities without restructuring your architecture 

Supporting where your business runs today, maintaining ful compatibility with where AI is going tomorrow. 

 5. You Establish a Foundation for Scalable, Repeatable AI Value 

Each new integration flow, each API, each event—becomes part of a reusable catalog.  
Over time, your organization builds a growing library of AI-ready building blocks that accelerate every future use case. 

This creates compounding value, year after year. 

What This Means for Your Business Today 

With the enhancements in this release, SAP Integration Suite becomes more than a technical enabler — it becomes your Enterprise circulatory system And, most importantly It becomes the key accelerator that turns AI ideas into enterprise-grade, production-ready outcomes. 

What’s Next? 

This release is a major step in SAP’s broader vision of building an enterprise-ready AI ecosystem where: 

  • Data flows freely but securely 
  • Systems speak the same language 
  • AI operates with governance and trust 
  • Autonomous agents can navigate complex end-to-end processes 

We’re excited to see how you apply these new capabilities to unlock efficiency, fuel innovation, and reshape how work gets done. 

 

More details can be found at:

Discovery Center

SAP Note  - Service Plans and Upgrade Paths 

SAP Integration Suite Roadmap

entry.title: Deep Dive: Essential Setup for S/4HANA and SAP Field Service Management Native Integration
entry.content:
Introduction

In today's fast-paced service landscape, the seamless flow of data between back-end ERP systems and front-line service operations is critical. The native integration between SAP S/4HANA and SAP Field Service Management (FSM) unlocks significant business value by enabling real-time master data synchronization, unified asset management, and end-to-end service order processing. By connecting these systems, organizations reduce manual data entry efforts, minimize errors, and ensure that field technicians have accurate, up-to-date information on equipment, customers, and spare parts.

This blog provides a deep technical walkthrough of the essential configuration steps required to set up this native integration. We will cover the complete landscape, starting from connectivity setup with Cloud Connector and authentication via OA2C, to configuring the Data Replication Framework (DRF), monitoring via the Application Interface Framework (AIF), setting up the Mobile Application Interface Framework (MAIF), and finalizing the FSM connector settings. Whether you are a technical consultant or an integration architect, this guide serves as a comprehensive reference for your implementation journey.

Integration Architecture Overview

The native integration leverages the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) as the integration middleware, enabling secure, bidirectional data flow between the on-premise S/4HANA system and the cloud-based Field Service Management solution. This architecture ensures robust connectivity through secure tunnels and utilizes standard, pre-built integration content.

Figure 1: Overview

Section 1: OA2C Configuration

OAuth 2.0 Client Credential Setup

This configuration is used for OAuth 2.0 client credential authentication setup between S/4HANA and SAP BTP services. It is a mandatory prerequisite before proceeding with other configurations.

Transaction Code: OA2C_CONFIG

Figure 2: OA2C Configuration page with FSM_PROFILE

 

Figure 3: Configure FSM_PROFILE with Client ID and Client Secret

Section 2: RFC Configuration

RFC (Remote Function Call) setup is the backbone of backend communication within the SAP landscape. Ensure that the RFC destinations are correctly configured to allow for seamless data exchange.

Figure 4: Create RFC of Type G

 

Figure 5: Enter the host name in RFC

Figure 6: Assign the OAuth Profile created in Section 1, to RFC

 Figure 7: Set the SSE Client PSE ID as Anonym SSL Client

Section 3: Cloud Connector Setup

The Cloud Connector serves as the secure tunnel between your on-premise S/4HANA system and the SAP BTP environment.

Access Link: https://<host>.cpi.c.eu-de-1.cloud.sap:8443/

  1. Log in to the Cloud Connector administration portal.
  2. Select Cloud to On-Premise from the menu to establish the tunnel mapping from SAP BTP to your S/4HANA backend system.
  3. Configure the internal host and port mapping to the virtual host and port.

Figure 8: Assign your cloud Connector in BTP

Figure 9: Cloud Connector Login Page

 

Figure 10: Select the BTP Sub Account

Figure 11: Configure Cloud to On-Premise with Hist Name and Authentication

Security Tip: 

Ensure that the system certificates are correctly exchanged between the Cloud Connector and the S/4HANA backend to prevent handshake errors during connectivity.

Section 4: SOAMANAGER Configuration

Transaction: SOAMANAGER

This configuration is critical for the setup of web services and is required for the following business objects:

  • Business Partner (BP)
  • BP Relationship
  • Product
  • Product Price
  • Product Stock

Figure 12: List of Objects that needs to be configured in SOAMANAGER

Configuration Steps:

  1. Create Manual Logical Port definitions for the consumer proxies.
  2. Set the newly created port as DEFAULT.
  3. Maintain technical user credentials for authentication.
  4. In the Transport Settings tab, enter the URL obtained after the iFlow deployment. This ensures that BP/SOAP events are correctly routed to Cloud Integration.

Figure 13: SOAMANAGER Home Screen: Select Web Service Configuration

Figure 14: In this example – Business Partner is configured

Figure 15: Create Manual Define logical ports and make the port as DEFAULT

 

Figure 16: Enter the Logical Port name

Figure 17: Enter technical user id for Authentication

Figure 18: Maintain the Messaging Tab as shown

Figure 19: Transport settings – mention the URL of obtained after iflow deployment

Note on iFlow URL

The iFlow URL must be obtained after deploying the relevant Integration Flow on SAP BTP Integration Suite. Configure this step after the endpoint is available.

Figure 20: End point URL of the IFLow after deployment

Section 5: Data Replication Framework (DRF)

DRF is the central tool used to distribute master and transactional data from S/4HANA to SAP FSM. It allows for flexible configuration of replication models and filters.

 Key Transactions

T-CodePurpose
DRFIMGCustomizing transaction to define replication models
DRFOUTTriggers the actual data replication (manual run)
DRFLOGViews logs to monitor data transfer status

 

Configuration Steps

  1. Define Business Systems: Navigate to create a new entry for the FSM target system

Figure 21: Define Business System

  1. Define Business Objects: Configure objects like Business Object 986 (Business Partner) and define what data entities should be replicated.

Figure 22: Define Business System

  1. Define Communication Channel: Set the Communication Channel to Replication via services.

Figure 23: Define Communication Channel

 

  1. Define Replication Models: Create a replication model that links the Business Object to the Target System.

Path - Data Replication -> Define Custom Settings for Data Replication -> Define Replication Models

  • Assign Outbound Implementation: Assign the relevant outbound implementation class to the model.

 

Figure 24: Assign Outbound Implementation

  • Assign Target Systems: Link the replication model to the specific FSM business system defined in step 1.

Figure 25: Assign Target Systems for Replication Model

  • Assign Outbound Parameters: Configure any additional parameters required for the replication logic.

Figure 26: Assign Outbound Parameter

  1. Activate the Replication Model: Finally, activate the model to enable live replication.

 

Figure 27: Activate Replication Model

 Testing Replication

Use T-Code   DRFOUT to manually trigger replication for testing purposes before scheduling background jobs. Use DRFLOG to inspect the results.

Section 6: AIF – SAP Application Interface Framework Monitoring

SAP AIF is used to monitor and manage integration messages between S/4HANA and FSM, providing a user-friendly interface for error handling.

Content Extraction

T-Code: /n /AIF/CONTENT_EXTRACT

 Extract the AIF content using the following scenario IDs:

Scenario IDIntegration Scope
SAP_AIF_0102Service Integration
SAP_AIF_0107Plant Maintenance Integration

Figure 28: Extract AIF Content

Viewing Loaded Content

Transaction Code - /n /AIF/CONTENT_DISP - Use this to view the loaded AIF Content after extraction.

Figure 29: Display extracted AIF Content

NOTE

Both SAP_AIF_01 02 and SAP_AIF_01 07 must be activated depending on your specific integration scope (Service vs. Plant Maintenance). Failure to activate the correct content will result in unmonitored messages.

 

Section 7: Customizing Configuration Values

This step involves maintaining specific configuration tables that control the behavior of the integration.

  • Maintain configuration tables with API details (Client ID, Client Secret, Auth URL).
  • Maintain DTO (Data Transfer Object) version settings to ensure compatibility between S/4HANA and FSM versions.
  • Save the configuration.

Figure 30: Integration Customizing Parameters

Figure 31: Maintain the parameter values as mentioned in help doc as a customizing entry

Section 8: Client Proxy Framework & Event Configuration

bgRFC Queue Setup

 Create bgRFC queues manually using T-Code SBGRFCCONF.  These queues are essential for handling background RFC processing for outbound events, ensuring that user transactions are not delayed by integration calls.

 

Figure 32 Maintain the bgRFC inbound destination manually

Section 9: Service Order Event Activation

Activate events in T-Code SWE2 . This configuration links the business object events (e.g., Service Order Created, Changed) to the receiver function modules that trigger the replication to FSM.

Figure 33: Activate events in SWE2

Troubleshooting Tip

Ensure all required event types are active in SWE2 before testing end-to-end replication. Missing event activations are a common root cause for failed replications where data changes in S/4HANA do not trigger any outbound message.

Section 10: MAIF – Mobile Application Interface Framework

MAIF is the framework required for replicating technical asset data. It is specifically used for the following object types:

  • Equipment
  • Functional Location
  • Characteristics
  • Classification

Configuration Steps:

  1. Activate BC Set: Use Transaction code SCPR20 to activate the BC Set /ACI/ASSET_CENTRAL_INTEGRATION and CRMS4_FSM_INTEGRATION_REF.
  2. Verify MDO Configuration: Navigate to Tcode -/n/SYCLO/CONFIGPANEL and verify that the Master Data Object (MDO) entries exist for the asset objects.Figure 34: Check if BC Set is activated
  3. Admin & Monitoring: - Use T-Code /n/SYCLO/ADMIN to maintain to maintain integration parameter details like API details, DTO versions etc.

    Figure 35: Maintain integration parameters in Additional Properties of Server Management

    Figure 36: Check Log in Inbound transaction Monitoring

Section 11: FSM Connector Settings (FSM → S/4HANA Inbound)

FSM Admin Portal

URL: https://<Cluster>.fsm.cloud.sap/admin

Routing iFlow

Configure the Routing iFlow in SAP BTP Integration Suite. This iFlow acts as the entry point from FSM to CPI to receive FSM event payloads. Events triggered in FSM (e.g., service confirmation, time recording) are received here and routed back to S/4HANA.

Figure 37: FSM Connector Configuration

 

Figure 38: Additional Configurations for different Objects

Error Monitoring & Reprocessing

Use the FSM Connector Error Monitoring tool for tracking failed inbound messages. This interface allows administrators to view error details and reprocess failed messages directly from the monitoring console.

Figure 39: FSM Connector Error Monitoring and Reprocessing

Conclusion

 

This blog covered the complete technical setup landscape for native S/4HANA-FSM integration, from authentication and connectivity (OA2C, Cloud Connector) through data replication frameworks (DRF, MAIF) to monitoring tools (AIF). Following these configuration steps ensures a robust, production-ready integration that supports seamless data consistency across your service operations.

As next steps, we recommend testing end-to-end scenarios—from master data replication to service order execution—while monitoring the data flow using AIF and FSM connector tools. Additionally, always keep your integration content updated by leveraging SAP's continuous updates to the BTP Integration Suite packages.

References & Useful Links

  • SAP Help Portal – Field Service Management Integration

entry.title: CI MCP Server: Managing SAP Cloud Integration with AI Assistants
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Cloud Integration (part of SAP Integration Suite) has a comprehensive OData V2 API that covers everything from integration content management to message monitoring, security material, and partner directory operations. But working with these APIs typically means writing scripts, using Postman, or navigating the Web UI manually.

What if you could simply ask your AI assistant: "Show me all failed messages from the last hour" or "Deploy the latest version of my iFlow"? That's what the CI MCP Server enables.

Built on the odata-mcp-proxy npm package, the CI MCP Server exposes all 32 entity sets of the SAP CPI OData V2 API as MCP tools,  with zero custom code. Read more about the odata-mcp-proxy here: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/odata-mcp-proxy-introduction/ba-p/14348684

What Can You Do With It?

The CI MCP Server gives AI assistants complete programmatic access to your SAP Cloud Integration tenant:

Integration Content Lifecycle

  • Create, read, update, and delete integration packages
  • Manage iFlows, value mappings, message mappings, and script collections
  • Track build and deploy status
  • View runtime artifact versions

Message Monitoring

  • Query message processing logs with rich filtering (status, date range, sender, receiver)
  • Drill into error details, attachments, and adapter attributes
  • Inspect custom header properties
  • Check exactly-once processing (ID mapping and idempotent repository)

Data Stores & Variables

  • Browse and delete data store entries
  • Inspect runtime variables
  • Monitor JMS queues (broker capacity, queue depth)

Security Material

  • Manage keystore entries, certificates, and SSH keys
  • CRUD operations on user credentials and OAuth2 client credentials
  • Manage secure parameters and certificate-to-user mappings
  • Configure access policies with artifact references

Partner Directory

  • Full CRUD on partners, string/binary parameters
  • Manage alternative partners and authorized users

API Categories & Entity Sets

The 32 entity sets are organized into 6 categories:

Category

Entity Sets

Description

integration-content

8

Packages, artifacts, build status, custom tags

message-processing-logs

3

Message logs with 5 navigation properties

message-stores

6

Data stores, variables, JMS queues, number ranges

log-files

2

Runtime logs and log archives

security-content

8

Keystores, credentials, certificates, access policies

partner-directory

5

Partners, parameters, authorized users

You can selectively enable categories using the ENABLED_API_CATEGORIES environment variable. For example, to only expose monitoring tools:

ENABLED_API_CATEGORIES=message-processing-logs,log-files

Configuration

The entire server is defined in ci-api-config.json:

{
  "server": {
    "name": "ci-mcp-server",
    "version": "1.0.0",
    "description": "SAP Cloud Integration MCP Server"
  },
  "apis": [{
    "name": "cpi",
    "destination": "CPI_DESTINATION",
    "pathPrefix": "/api/v1",
    "csrfProtected": true,
    "entitySets": [
      {
        "entitySet": "IntegrationPackages",
        "description": "Integration packages ...",
        "category": "integration-content",
        "keys": [{ "name": "Id", "type": "string" }],
        "operations": { "list": true, "get": true, "create": true, "update": true, "delete": true },
        "filterableProperties": ["Id", "Name", "Vendor", "Mode", "Version"]
      },
      ... (32 entity sets total)
    ]
  }]
}

Key configuration choices:

  • csrfProtected: true — SAP CPI uses OData V2 with CSRF token protection. The proxy automatically fetches and manages CSRF tokens for all mutating operations (POST, PATCH, DELETE).
  • Single destination — All 32 entity sets share one CPI tenant destination with the /api/v1 path prefix.
  • Navigation properties — MessageProcessingLogs has 5 navigation properties (Attachments, ErrorInformations, AdapterAttributes, CustomHeaderProperties, MessageStoreEntries), each generating an additional _list tool for drilling into related data.

Setting Up the BTP Destination

Create a destination in your SAP BTP Cockpit:

Property

Value

Name

CPI_DESTINATION

Type

HTTP

URL

https://<tenant>.it-cpi018.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com

Authentication

OAuth2ClientCredentials

Client ID

From your CPI service key

Client Secret

From your CPI service key

Token Service URL

https://<subdomain>.authentication.<region>.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token

To get the service key credentials, create a service instance of "Process Integration Runtime" with plan "api" in your BTP subaccount, then create a service key.

Local Development

For local testing without BTP, set environment variables:

CPI_DESTINATION_BASE_URL=https://tenant.it-cpi018.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com
CPI_DESTINATION_TOKEN_URL=https://subdomain.authentication.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token
CPI_DESTINATION_CLIENT_ID=your-client-id
CPI_DESTINATION_CLIENT_SECRET=your-client-secret

Or use the default-env.json with the destination from BTP:

{
  "VCAP_SERVICES": {
    "xsuaa": [
      {
        "name": "mcp-uaa",
        "label": "xsuaa",
        "tags": ["xsuaa"],
        "credentials":<btp xssuaa credentials>
      }
    ],
    "destination": [
      {
        "name": "mcp-dest",
        "label": "destination",
        "tags": ["destination"],
        "credentials":<btp destination credentials>
      }
    ],
    "connectivity": [
      {
        "name": "mcp-conn",
        "label": "connectivity",
        "tags": ["connectivity"],
        "credentials": <btp connectivity credentials>
      }
    ]
  }
}

Then run:

npm install
npm start

Deploying to SAP BTP

# Build MTA archive
npm run build:btp

# Deploy to Cloud Foundry
npm run deploy:btp

The MTA deployment creates three service instances (Destination, Connectivity, XSUAA) and binds them to your application. The health check endpoint at /health is used by Cloud Foundry to monitor application status.

Example Conversations

Here are some example prompts you can use with AI assistants:

User: "Show me all integration packages"
AI: Calls IntegrationPackages_list

User: "What iFlows are in the SAP_HCI_Monitoring package?"
AI: Calls IntegrationPackages_IntegrationDesigntimeArtifacts_list
    with path: "('SAP_HCI_Monitoring')"

User: "Show me failed messages from the last 24 hours"
AI: Calls MessageProcessingLogs_list
    with path: "?$filter=Status eq 'FAILED' and LogEnd gt datetime'2026-03-11T00:00:00'"

User: "What's the error for message ID abc123?"
AI: Calls MessageProcessingLogs_ErrorInformations_list
    with path: "('abc123')"

Project Structure

ci-mcp-server/
├── ci-api-config.json      # All 32 entity set definitions
├── package.json            # Single dependency: odata-mcp-proxy
├── mta.yaml                # BTP Cloud Foundry deployment
├── xs-security.json        # XSUAA roles (Viewer/Editor/Admin)
└── default-env.json        # Local dev credentials (gitignored)

Links

  • CI MCP Server (GitHub): https://github.com/lemaiwo/ci-mcp-server
  • OData MCP Proxy (npm): https://www.npmjs.com/package/odata-mcp-proxy
  • OData MCP Proxy (GitHub): https://github.com/lemaiwo/odata-mcp-proxy
  • SAP CPI OData API Reference: SAP Business Accelerator Hub

entry.title: DEIP — Domain Enterprise Integration Pattern: A Semantic Control Plane for Enterprise Integration
entry.content:
Disclaimer

Between March 12 and 13, 2026, during a late-night architecture review, the model I had been developing finally crystallized.
What had initially started as two SAP-oriented patterns converged into a five-layer semantic integration architecture, culminating in

DEIP – Domain Enterprise Integration Pattern.

On February 7, 2026 the scope of this work was limited to two patterns designed for SAP landscapes:

  • DCRP – Domain-Centric Routing Pattern
  • PDCP – Package Domain-Centric Pattern

Both focused on solving structural governance problems in SAP API Management and SAP Cloud Integration.

In just 36 days this initial nucleus evolved into a fully formalized five-layer semantic control architecture, designed to address a problem that many integration architects recognize but rarely formalize: integration sprawl.

This problem is not new.

For 17 years I have observed the same structural pattern repeating itself across different generations of SAP integration technologies:

  • SAP XI – fragmented Software Components
  • SAP PI/PO – interface and configuration proliferation
  • SAP BTP Integration Suite – proxy, package, credential and artifact sprawl inside CPI landscapes
  • SAP API Management - Proxy Sprawl Apocalypse
  • EDA and SOLACE in the same landscape for event mesh, what ? Serio ?
  • Others.....

Each generation improved the technology stack, but the structural indexing problem remained the same: integrations were organized by system or project, rather than by domain intent.

During these 36 days of research and experimentation, this recurring chaos was distilled into a single architectural model.

At the center of this model sits DEIP, the parent architecture layer responsible for governing the three execution patterns:

DEIP — Semantic Control Plane Decision Model
└── Decision Pipeline
       → Intent
       → Interaction Type
       → Quality Profile
       → Integration Capability
       → Protocol Profile
       → Platform Binding
    └── SDIA — Semantic Domain Integration Architecture
           (DEIP Reference Architecture)
        ├── GDCR — Gateway Domain-Centric Routing
        │        Reference Implementation — recommended starting point
        ├── DDCR — Domain Driven-Centric Router
        │        Routing and capability resolution engine
        └── ODCP — Orchestration Domain Composition Pattern
                 Cross-domain orchestration layer
     └── Execution Platforms

    SAP BTP · AWS · Azure · Kong · Kafka · IoT · AI/LLM · etc.

Together, these patterns form a domain-centric semantic control plane that sits above any specific execution platform.

From this point of closure — looking at SAP landscapes where the required capabilities already exist but are often applied without consistent domain semantics — this final blog post of the series begins.

Disclaimer SAP

SAP BTP Integration Suite already provides all the capabilities necessary to implement and validate this architecture in practice.

The platform offers:

  • a mature API Management layer
  • a powerful Cloud Integration runtime
  • Event Mesh
  • security services
  • and strong governance tooling.

The problem has never been the SAP platform itself.

Instead, the issue often lies in how integrations are indexed and governed.Many landscapes organize integrations by projects and systems, rather than by domain intent, which leads to proxy, package and credential sprawl even on top of a strong platform.

This same concern was raised earlier in January 2026 in my SAP Community article:

 Clean Architecture vs The Frankenstein Landscape

This blog originated from a situation of chaos that I observed in several customer landscapes.

The SAP BTP Integration Suite was available, but the way integration responsibilities were distributed made the architecture unnecessarily fragmented.

In some environments the pattern looked like this:

  • SAP API Management used only for standard APIs

  • SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) used only for standard packages

  • MuleSoft API-Led used for custom APIs

  • Boomi acting as a cloud middleware for custom integrations

And that was only part of the picture.

In many landscapes additional platforms were also introduced for other integration styles:

  • Event Mesh / Event Brokers

  • Kafka

  • IBM MQ

  • IoT platforms

  • Queue-based middleware

  • Streaming platforms

Each new integration requirement often resulted in another platform, another gateway, or another integration layer.

When I saw these architectures repeating themselves across different customers, a simple question came to my mind:

Is this really serious architecture?

Does this actually make sense from an integration perspective ? 

A kind of Frankenstein landscape built from overlapping platforms and responsibilities?

For me, the answer was no.

In most cases this situation is not driven by real technical constraints.
It is usually the result of organizational fragmentation, vendor tendency, or what I call “architects by manual” — architects who simply follow platform playbooks instead of questioning the architecture itself.

In many cases the result looks like multiple platforms overlapping while performing essentially the same functions.

The landscape becomes fragmented across different technologies and integration styles: APIs, events, queues, messaging platforms and middleware layers. What should be a coherent integration architecture slowly turns into a patchwork of tools and responsibilities.

This creates several structural problems.

There is multi-protocol exposure without clear consolidation, where the same business capability appears through different protocols such as REST, SOAP, OData, BAPI, RFC or IDoc, often managed by separate proxies and platforms.

At the same time, there is no centralized governance for integration decisions. Each team chooses tools, protocols or patterns independently, which leads to architectural inconsistency.

The consequences are predictable:

  • overlapping platforms performing the same functions

  • lack of centralized governance

  • high maintenance cost

  • complex operational landscapes

  • fragmented teams with different platform specializations

  • limited collaboration around the business domain itself

Instead of organizing integration around the domain, the architecture becomes organized around tools and vendors.

From my perspective, this is where the real problem begins.

When I look at these landscapes today, after studying integration structures more deeply, I often see something that resembles an architectural collapse in slow motion. Too many tools, too many integration surfaces, and too little structural coherence.

That realization pushed me to start researching this problem more seriously.

I kept studying, experimenting, and trying to understand how integration architecture could be organized from a more fundamental perspective.

One of the questions that kept coming back was simple:

Do we really need six different proxies for the same capability?

If the same business capability is exposed through multiple protocols – for example REST, SOAP and OData – does it really need to result in separate integration surfaces every time? Or can we centralize these capabilities behind a smaller number of domain‑driven HTTPS entry points, instead of multiplying proxies and integration layers across platforms ? 

And if we look one level deeper, the same question applies to the underlying protocols themselves.

Why do we need so many protocols for the same domain behaviour? In many cases, BAPIs, RFCs and IDocs can be exposed as HTTPS web services (REST, SOAP or OData) behind a single semantic façade.

Centralizing domain capabilities over HTTPS does not remove backend diversity, but it drastically reduces the number of exposed surfaces and makes the integration landscape governable again.

The same question applies to SAP Cloud Integration.

Do we really need dozens of CPI packages and technical users for the same domain?

If one business domain is implemented across multiple backends – SAP S/4HANA, Salesforce, legacy ERPs, logistics providers – does it really require a separate CPI package and a new technical user for every single interface and project?

Or can we consolidate these flows into a small number of domain‑centric packages, with tiered technical users aligned to sub‑domains, instead of multiplying packages and credentials until the landscape becomes ungovernable?

These questions eventually led me to formalize the architecture that I now call:

DEIP — Domain Enterprise Integration Pattern.

DEIP was designed precisely to address this type of architectural fragmentation by starting from the domain, rather than from the technology or the platform.

From there, the integration architecture can be derived in a deterministic way.

My research is not perfect and it is still evolving, but the goal is simple: to provide a starting point for bringing structural coherence back into integration architectures.

Wherever I work, that is the direction I intend to follow — organizing integration around the domain, not around fragmented platforms.

As a way of acknowledging the SAP ecosystem that shaped much of this work over the past 17 years, I am making a small professional commitment.

Based on my experience working with several API management platforms – including five different API gateways during the research – SAP API Management stands out for its user interface, configuration simplicity and proxy creation experience.

However, an important realization emerged during this research: when a  DDCR (Domain Driver Centric Router) is introduced, the role of the API gateway itself changes significantly.

At that point, the gateway is no longer just a platform for exposing and managing URLs.

It becomes part of a metadata‑driven control plane, where the final destination is resolved deterministic dynamically from domain intent, metadata and lightweight routing logic.In practical terms, this means that any API management platform – regardless of vendor – can operate at the same architectural level when combined with DDCR.

The gateway stops being merely a URL manager and becomes a decoupling layer between intent and destination, resolved through metadata and deterministic routing.


In this model, SAP API Management evolves from a traditional API gateway into a domain‑driven integration entry point, where routing decisions are driven by the control plane rather than statically defined inside individual proxies.

Alignment with SAP Strategic Directions

This work aligns directly with three strategic directions already promoted by SAP:

  • Clean Core
    • DEIP keeps protocol complexity and integration decision logic outside of S/4HANA by moving these concerns into a semantic control plane on SAP BTP.
    • This allows the core ERP to remain standard while integrations stay governed and evolvable.
  • RISE with SAP
    • By indexing integrations on domain intent rather than on individual systems, DEIP provides RISE transformation programs with a structured way to rationalize legacy interfaces during cloud migrations.
  • ISA-M
    • DEIP complements ISA-M by operating as a decision layer above the integration scenario model.While ISA-M classifies integration scenarios, DEIP maps them to capabilities, protocol profiles and platform bindings, ensuring that integration architectures remain domain-driven and consistent across platforms.
